**Step 4 — Choose a lab-friendly value:** A common Arduino sampling rate is **250 Hz** (using `delay(4)`), which lies safely above the Nyquist limit and is achievable with stable serial output.

**Verification — Samples per QRS:**

$$N_{QRS} = T_{QRS} \times f_s = 0.1 \times 250 = 25 \text{ samples per QRS}$$

This resolution is more than sufficient for a clean waveform.

*(7 marks — 2 for statement, 2 for computation, 1 for safety margin, 1 for verification, 1 for conclusion.)*

#### **Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Explain the various sources of noise in ECG acquisition and how the AD8232 + Arduino combination mitigates them.

**Model Solution:**

| Noise Source | Frequency / Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power-line interference (50 Hz India / 60 Hz USA)** | Large common-mode, ~mV at skin | **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** amplifier of AD8232 injects inverted common-mode back into the body; combined with the IA's CMRR > 80 dB |
| **DC Electrode Offset** | Up to ±300 mV (half-cell potential of Ag/AgCl) | **High-Pass Filter** in AD8232 (cut-off 0.5 Hz) blocks DC and very slow baseline wander |
| **Muscle artefact (EMG)** | High frequency, broadband | **Low-Pass Filter** in AD8232 (cut-off 40 Hz) attenuates EMG noise above the ECG band |
| **Baseline wander** | < 0.5 Hz (respiration, motion) | HPF at 0.5 Hz attenuates this; further reduced by software moving-average filter in Arduino |
| **Quantisation noise** | ≤ LSB/2 = 2.44 mV | Use full ADC range (0–3.3 V) so that the amplified ECG occupies most of the input span; 10-bit resolution is adequate |
| **Radio-frequency interference** | > 1 MHz | Shielded electrode cables; the AD8232's input EMI filter |
| **Thermal / Johnson noise** | µV level | Low source impedance of the IA; usually negligible vs. ECG |
| **Aliasing** | All frequencies fold | Sampling at $f_s = 250$ Hz ensures the LPF at 40 Hz has already removed frequencies above $f_s/2 = 125$ Hz |

**Conclusion:** The AD8232's integrated front-end + Arduino's 10-bit ADC + a 250 Hz sampling rate collectively yield a clean, real-time ECG trace that is sufficient for educational, hobby, and basic clinical-grade monitoring applications.

*(7 marks — 1 for each of 6 noise sources + 1 for the integrated conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in this experiment (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Wrong supply voltage to AD8232.** The AD8232 is a **3.3 V device**. Connecting it to the Arduino's **5 V pin will permanently damage the chip**. Always use the **3.3 V** pin.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use `delay()`** in the firmware. Without `delay`, the analogRead runs as fast as ~9.6 kHz and floods the serial port, causing the Serial Plotter to freeze or display a flat line. The correct value is `delay(4)` for 250 Hz.
>
> 3. **Confusing 5V TTL with 3.3V logic on LO+/LO- pins.** Although the LO- outputs are 3.3 V, the Arduino's analog inputs tolerate this without a level shifter. However, students often unnecessarily add level shifters and lose marks on the "simplicity" criterion.
>
> 4. **Wrong electrode placement.** Placing RA and LA on the *same side* of the body (or both on the chest close to each other) yields a tiny differential voltage and a noisy/invisible QRS. Always place them on **opposite sides** of the heart.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the sampling theorem** when justifying the 250 Hz choice. Examiners specifically award marks for the **Nyquist justification** — never write "I used delay(4)" without explaining *why* 4 ms is the correct choice.
>
> 6. **Skipping lead-off detection in the record.** Even if not implemented in the code, the report **must mention** the LOP/LON pins and their function.
>
> 7. **Reporting BPM without a clear formula.** Always show:
> $$\text{BPM} = \frac{60\,000 \text{ ms}}{T_{RR} \text{ in ms}}$$
> A bare number without a derivation loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 8. **Confusing gain of 100 (AD8232) with ADC resolution (1024).** These are completely different quantities. AD8232 gain is in V/V; ADC resolution is in bits.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Tick these off before entering the exam hall / lab:**

- [ ] **ECG definition** — recording of cardiac electrical activity via skin electrodes.
- [ ] **ECG amplitude:** **0.5–5 mV** | **ECG bandwidth (clinical):** **0.5–40 Hz** | **Full diagnostic:** **0.05–150 Hz**.
- [ ] **P wave** → atrial depolarisation (60–110 ms).
- [ ] **QRS complex** → ventricular depolarisation (80–120 ms), peak ~1 mV.
- [ ] **T wave** → ventricular repolarisation (~160 ms).
- [ ] **AD8232 is a 3.3 V analog front end** with **gain = 100 V/V** and **CMRR > 80 dB**.
- [ ] **Three electrodes** → RA, LA, RL (Lead II equivalent of Einthoven's triangle).
- [ ] **Right-Leg Drive (RLD)** actively cancels **50 Hz** mains hum.
- [ ] **Arduino UNO ADC** is **10-bit**, default **Vref = 5 V**, **LSB = 4.88 mV**.
- [ ] **Nyquist rate** for ECG = **2 × 40 = 80 Hz**; practical rate = **250 Hz** (delay 4 ms).
- [ ] **Baud rate** = **9600** for reliable Serial Plotter display.
- [ ] **Sampling formula:** $T = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ ; **Samples per QRS:** $N = T_{QRS} \times f_s$.
- [ ] **BPM formula:** $\text{BPM} = \dfrac{60\,000}{T_{RR} \text{ (ms)}}$.
- [ ] **Voltage reconstruction:** $V_{in} = D \times \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1024}$.
- [ ] **Voltage at skin:** $V_{skin} = \dfrac{V_{Arduino}}{G_{AD8232}} = \dfrac{V_{Arduino}}{100}$.
- [ ] **Serial Plotter** displays integer values vs. time automatically; no extra libraries needed.
- [ ] **Lead-off detection** uses LOP / LON pins connected to Arduino A1 / A2.
- [ ] **Always power the AD8232 from the 3.3 V pin — never 5 V.**
- [ ] **Common lab results:** clean ECG visible in <10 s, BPM typically 70–90 in healthy resting students.
- [ ] **KTU viva favourites:** "What is CMRR?", "Why 0.5 Hz HPF?", "Why 40 Hz LPF?", "What happens if we sample at 50 Hz?" (aliasing!), "What is Einthoven's triangle?"

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "E-C-G-A-D-S" for the complete signal chain:**
> **E**lectrodes → **C**onditioning (AD8232) → **G**ain ×100 → **A**DC (Arduino 10-bit) → **D**isplay (Serial Plotter) → **S**ave (optional file log).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UJT Relaxation Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL507 - MEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** UJT Relaxation Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **UJT Relaxation Oscillator** is a non-linear, free-running oscillator circuit that uses a **Unijunction Transistor (UJT)** as the active switching element along with a resistor–capacitor (RC) timing network. It generates a **sawtooth (ramp) waveform** across the timing capacitor and sharp **trigger pulses** across the Base-1 resistor, exploiting the negative-resistance region of the UJT's emitter characteristics. It is classified as a *relaxation-type oscillator* because the energy stored in the capacitor is periodically released (relaxed) through the UJT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A UJT Relaxation Oscillator is a square-loop oscillator where the period of oscillation is governed by the exponential charging of a capacitor through a resistor up to the UJT's *Peak Point Voltage (VP)*, followed by rapid discharge through the UJT.

## 1.2 The Unijunction Transistor (UJT) — The Heart of the Circuit

The UJT is a three-terminal, single-junction solid-state device consisting of an N-type silicon bar with two ohmic base contacts (B1 and B2) at opposite ends, and a small P-type emitter alloyed laterally into the bar.

- **Emitter (E):** the only P-region; it forms a single PN junction with the bar.
- **Base 1 (B1):** the lower-base terminal, tied to the lower potential.
- **Base 2 (B2):** the upper-base terminal, tied to the higher potential.
- The bar between B1 and B2 acts as a voltage divider with internal resistances **RB1** and **RB2** such that **RBB = RB1 + RB2**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identity:** Despite having one junction, the UJT is *not* a diode. It exhibits a controlled, regenerative negative-resistance switching behaviour governed by its **Intrinsic Standoff Ratio (η)**, typically between **0.51 and 0.82**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tipping Bucket" 🌧️

Imagine a **tipping-bucket rain gauge**:

- **Raindrops** falling into the bucket are like electrons charging the **capacitor (C)** through the **resistor (R)** from the supply **VBB**.
- When the bucket becomes **full**, it tips over and dumps all the water in one go.
- In our oscillator, when the capacitor voltage **Vc** rises to the critical **Peak Voltage (VP)**, the UJT suddenly "tips" — its emitter-to-B1 resistance collapses, dumping the capacitor's charge through **RB1** almost instantaneously.
- The capacitor then empties down to a low **Valley Voltage (VV)**, the UJT switches OFF, and the next raindrop-filling cycle begins.

This **slow fill, fast dump** behaviour is precisely what produces a **sawtooth waveform** — the foundation of medical time-base generators, pacemakers, and SCR/MOSFET trigger circuits.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world medical relevance:** The UJT relaxation oscillator is used in **defibrillator synchronisation circuits**, **patient monitoring SCR-trigger networks**, and **digital thermistor-based respiration rate monitors** in the KTU 2024 Medical Electronics curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction & Equivalent Circuit of UJT

The UJT can be modelled as a **variable resistance RBB = RB1 + RB2** with a fictitious diode D at the emitter, whose anode is connected to the tap point of the internal divider. The intrinsic standoff ratio is:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1}+R_{B2}} \;=\; \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of η:** It is the fraction of the inter-base voltage (VBB) that would appear across RB1 *if no emitter current flows*. It is a **purely geometric/constructional** parameter set at the factory, typically **0.6 to 0.75** for 2N2646 (the most common UJT in KTU labs).

## 2.2 Operation of the Relaxation Oscillator — Step-by-Step Logic

**Circuit topology:** R (timing resistor) is connected between VBB and the Emitter (E). Capacitor C is from E to ground. RB1 is from B1 to ground. RB2 is from B2 to VBB. The UJT's B2 is biased positive with respect to B1.

| Step | Circuit State | UJT Condition | Capacitor Behaviour |
|:----:|:--------------|:--------------|:--------------------|
| **1. Charging Phase** | Vc < VP | UJT OFF (cut-off) | C charges from VBB through R: Vc rises **exponentially** |
| **2. Peak Firing** | Vc = VP | UJT fires (ON) | E–B1 junction forward biased; R-E region saturates |
| **3. Discharging Phase** | Vc > VV | UJT in negative-resistance region | C discharges rapidly through UJT and RB1 |
| **4. Valley Cut-off** | Vc = VV | UJT turns OFF | Emitter current IE < IV (valley current) |
| **5. Recycle** | Vc < VP | Back to Step 1 | Next oscillation cycle begins |

## 2.3 Critical Voltages — The "Why" Behind Each

- **Peak Point Voltage (VP):** the voltage at which the UJT fires.
- **Valley Point Voltage (VV):** the voltage at which the UJT switches off.
- **Peak Point Current (IP):** the minimum emitter current to fire the UJT (typically **2 to 25 µA**).
- **Valley Point Current (IV):** the current below which the UJT cannot remain ON (typically **1 to 25 mA**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the UJT fire at VP?**
> The voltage at the internal P-region tap is **η · VBB**. The diode D turns ON only when Vc exceeds (η·VBB + VD), where VD is the forward diode drop (~0.6 to 0.7 V). Once ON, holes are injected into the N-bar, modulating RB1 downward, which raises the tap voltage — a **regenerative** effect that latches the UJT ON.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Typical Value | Notes |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:----------------------|:------|
| 1 | Intrinsic Standoff Ratio | $\eta = R_{B1} \div (R_{B1} + R_{B2})$ | dimensionless, 0.51–0.82 | Set by manufacturer |
| 2 | Peak Point Voltage | $V_{P} = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_{D}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{D} \approx 0.7$ V |
| 3 | Valley Point Voltage | $V_{V}$ (from datasheet) | Volts (V) | e.g., 2N2646 → 1–3 V |
| 4 | Charging Time Constant | $\tau_{c} = R \cdot C$ | seconds (s) | Determines T1 |
| 5 | Time Period (approx) | $T \approx R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)$ | seconds (s) | Dominant charging time |
| 6 | Frequency of Oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Output sawtooth frequency |
| 7 | Pulse Amplitude at RB1 | $V_{RB1} \approx V_{P}$ | Volts (V) | Sharp positive spike |
| 8 | Energy per Pulse | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{P}^{2} - V_{V}^{2})$ | Joules (J) | Discharge through RB1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Design Constraint:** The timing resistor **R** must satisfy **R_min < R < R_max** to guarantee oscillation. R_min = (VBB − VV) ⁄ IV and R_max = (VBB − VP) ⁄ IP. Choosing R outside this range leads to either latch-up or no oscillation — a **favourite KTU viva question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Electronics:** SCR-trigger pulses for **DC defibrillators** and **thyristor-based motor drives** in hospital ventilators.
- **Industrial Control:** Phase-control firing circuits for TRIACs in **biomedical incubator temperature regulation**.
- **Telecommunication:** Sawtooth time-base generators in **CRT patient monitors** (legacy systems still referenced in KTU textbooks).
- **Power Electronics:** Triggering of IGBTs in **switched-mode power supplies (SMPS)** for MRI and CT equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Oscillation Time Period (T)

### Step A — Equation of Capacitor Voltage During Charging

When the UJT is OFF, the capacitor C charges from supply VBB through resistor R. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop (VBB → R → C → ground):

$$
V_{BB} \;=\; i \cdot R \;+\; V_{C}(t)
$$

where the charging current is $i = C \cdot \dfrac{dV_{C}}{dt}$. Substituting:

$$
V_{BB} \;=\; R \cdot C \cdot \frac{dV_{C}}{dt} \;+\; V_{C}(t)
$$

Rearranging into standard first-order form:

$$
R \cdot C \cdot \frac{dV_{C}}{dt} \;+\; V_{C}(t) \;=\; V_{BB}
$$

Dividing throughout by $R \cdot C$:

$$
\frac{dV_{C}}{dt} \;+\; \frac{1}{R C} \, V_{C}(t) \;=\; \frac{V_{BB}}{R C}
$$

### Step B — Solving the First-Order Linear Differential Equation

Using the standard integrating-factor method with $IF = e^{t/RC}$, the general solution (assuming initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$ at the start of the charging phase) is:

$$
V_{C}(t) \;=\; V_{BB} \left(1 - e^{-t / (R C)}\right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the classic form $y(t) = y_{\infty} - (y_{\infty} - y_{0}) e^{-t/\tau}$, where $y_{\infty} = V_{BB}$, $y_{0} = 0$, and $\tau = RC$. The capacitor asymptotically approaches VBB but is interrupted by the UJT firing.

### Step C — Finding the Charging Time T1 up to the Peak Point

At the instant of firing, $V_C(t) = V_P$. Substituting into the charging equation:

$$
V_{P} \;=\; V_{BB} \left(1 - e^{-T_{1} / (R C)}\right)
$$

Solving for the exponential term:

$$
e^{-T_{1} / (R C)} \;=\; 1 - \frac{V_{P}}{V_{BB}} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{V_{BB}}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
-\frac{T_{1}}{R C} \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{V_{BB}}\right)
$$

Hence the **charging (slow) time**:

$$
T_{1} \;=\; R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)
$$

### Step D — Discharge Time T2 Through the UJT

During discharge, the UJT presents a very small dynamic resistance (typically 5–25 Ω). The discharge path is through the UJT and the parallel combination of RB1 and the on-state resistance. The discharge is very rapid (microseconds) and is often neglected in first-order KTU design problems. The discharge time is:

$$
T_{2} \;=\; R_{d} \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{P}}{V_{V}}\right)
$$

where $R_d$ is the small discharge resistance (≈ on-state UJT resistance + RB1).

### Step E — Net Time Period and Frequency

The total period of one complete oscillation cycle is:

$$
T \;=\; T_{1} \;+\; T_{2}
$$

$$
T \;=\; R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The combined form arises because $\ln(A \cdot B) = \ln A + \ln B$, where $A = V_{BB}/(V_{BB}-V_P)$ from charging and $B = V_P/V_V$ from discharge. The frequency of oscillation is therefore:

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{T} \;=\; \frac{1}{R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Given:** A UJT relaxation oscillator uses a 2N2646 UJT with $\eta = 0.65$, $V_{BB} = 12$ V, $V_D = 0.7$ V, $V_V = 2$ V, $R = 50$ kΩ, $C = 0.1$ µF.

**Find:** (a) Peak point voltage, (b) Time period of oscillation, (c) Frequency.

### Part (a) — Peak Point Voltage

Substituting into the peak-voltage formula:

$$
V_{P} \;=\; \eta \cdot V_{BB} \;+\; V_{D}
$$

$$
V_{P} \;=\; (0.65)(12) \;+\; 0.7
$$

$$
V_{P} \;=\; 7.8 \;+\; 0.7 \;=\; 8.5 \text{ V}
$$

### Part (b) — Time Period

First compute the denominator expression $V_{BB} - V_P$:

$$
V_{BB} - V_{P} \;=\; 12 - 8.5 \;=\; 3.5 \text{ V}
$$

Next compute $V_{BB} - V_V$:

$$
V_{BB} - V_{V} \;=\; 12 - 2 \;=\; 10 \text{ V}
$$

The ratio inside the logarithm is:

$$
\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}} \;=\; \frac{10}{3.5} \;\approx\; 2.857
$$

The natural logarithm is:

$$
\ln(2.857) \;\approx\; 1.0498
$$

The RC product is:

$$
R C \;=\; (50 \times 10^{3}) \times (0.1 \times 10^{-6}) \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 5 \text{ ms}
$$

Hence the time period:

$$
T \;=\; (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.0498)
$$

$$
T \;\approx\; 5.249 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;\approx\; 5.25 \text{ ms}
$$

### Part (c) — Frequency

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{T} \;=\; \frac{1}{5.249 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f \;\approx\; 190.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Result Summary Table**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:---------|:------:|:------|:-----|
| Peak Point Voltage | $V_P$ | **8.5** | V |
| Time Period | $T$ | **5.25** | ms |
| Frequency | $f$ | **190.5** | Hz |
| RC Time Constant | $\tau$ | **5.0** | ms |

## 3.3 Derivation of the Minimum and Maximum Timing Resistance

For **sustained oscillation**, the load line drawn on the UJT's emitter V–I curve must intersect the negative-resistance region. This imposes the dual constraint:

**Maximum R (oscillation must start):** The current supplied through R at $V_C = V_P$ must be at least equal to $I_P$:

$$
R_{max} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{I_{P}}
$$

**Minimum R (oscillation must not latch):** The current supplied through R at $V_C = V_V$ must be less than $I_V$ to allow the UJT to switch OFF:

$$
R_{min} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{I_{V}}
$$

The condition for **sustained oscillation** is therefore:

$$
\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{I_{V}} \;<\; R \;<\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{I_{P}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The upper bound ensures the DC load line is *flatter* than the UJT's cut-off characteristic (avoids failure to fire), while the lower bound ensures the load line is *steeper* than the negative-resistance slope (avoids latch-up). This is a **direct application of the load-line stability criterion** in NDR (negative differential resistance) devices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Intrinsic Standoff Ratio" (η) of a UJT and state its typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** The intrinsic standoff ratio $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of the resistance of the base-1 region (RB1) to the total inter-base resistance (RBB = RB1 + RB2) of the UJT.
- (1 mark) **Mathematical form:** $\eta = R_{B1} / (R_{B1} + R_{B2})$
- (1 mark) **Typical range and significance:** $\eta$ lies between **0.51 and 0.82** for commercial UJTs (e.g., 2N2646 has $\eta \approx 0.65$). It is a **construction-dependent** parameter that determines the **peak-point firing voltage** of the UJT.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Why is the UJT relaxation oscillator called a "relaxation" oscillator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) A relaxation oscillator is one in which energy is **stored slowly** in a reactive element (the capacitor C) and **released abruptly** through a switching device (the UJT).
- (1 mark) The term "relaxation" refers to the periodic **relaxation of the capacitor's stored charge** through the UJT's negative-resistance region, producing a non-sinusoidal (sawtooth) waveform.
- (1 mark) Unlike sinusoidal LC oscillators, relaxation oscillators generate **relaxation cycles** with abrupt state transitions, used primarily as **time-base generators** and **trigger pulse sources** in medical and industrial electronics.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a UJT relaxation oscillator. Draw the waveforms at the capacitor and at the Base-1 resistor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A UJT relaxation oscillator uses a 2N2646 with $\eta = 0.6$, $V_{BB} = 18$ V, $V_D = 0.6$ V, $V_V = 1.5$ V, $R = 100$ kΩ, and $C = 0.05$ µF. Calculate the peak-point voltage, time period, and frequency of oscillation.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (2 marks):** The circuit consists of a DC supply VBB feeding a timing resistor R connected to the UJT emitter E. A timing capacitor C is connected from E to ground. The UJT has its B2 tied to VBB through a small bias resistor RB2 (≈ 100 Ω), and B1 connected to ground through an output resistor RB1 (≈ 100 Ω).

**Working (3 marks):**
- **Phase 1 (Charging):** Initially, UJT is OFF. Capacitor C charges from VBB through R following the exponential law $V_C(t) = V_{BB}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.
- **Phase 2 (Firing):** When $V_C$ reaches the peak-point voltage $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$, the emitter–base-1 junction becomes forward-biased, the UJT fires, and the dynamic emitter resistance drops sharply.
- **Phase 3 (Discharging):** Capacitor discharges rapidly through the UJT and RB1, producing a sharp positive pulse of amplitude ≈ $V_P$ across RB1.
- **Phase 4 (Cut-off):** When $V_C$ falls to the valley voltage $V_V$, the UJT turns off and the cycle restarts.

**Waveforms (2 marks):**
- Across the **capacitor**: a **sawtooth wave** (slow exponential rise, fast exponential fall).
- Across **RB1**: a series of **narrow positive pulses** synchronised with each firing instant.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Peak Point Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{P} \;=\; \eta \cdot V_{BB} \;+\; V_{D}
$$

$$
V_{P} \;=\; (0.6)(18) \;+\; 0.6 \;=\; 10.8 \;+\; 0.6 \;=\; 11.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Computing Logarithmic Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
V_{BB} - V_{P} \;=\; 18 - 11.4 \;=\; 6.6 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{BB} - V_{V} \;=\; 18 - 1.5 \;=\; 16.5 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}} \;=\; \frac{16.5}{6.6} \;=\; 2.5
$$

$$
\ln(2.5) \;\approx\; 0.9163
$$

**Step 3 — RC Time Constant and Time Period (2 marks):**

$$
R \cdot C \;=\; (100 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (0.05 \times 10^{-6}) \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;=\; 5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T \;=\; R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right) \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9163
$$

$$
T \;\approx\; 4.58 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \;\approx\; 4.58 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency (1 mark):**

$$
f \;=\; \frac{1}{T} \;=\; \frac{1}{4.58 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 218.3 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Peak Voltage $V_P$ | **11.4 V** |
| Time Period $T$ | **4.58 ms** |
| Frequency $f$ | **218.3 Hz** |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the time period of oscillation of a UJT relaxation oscillator. State the conditions for **sustained oscillation** in terms of R_min and R_max.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a UJT relaxation oscillator to generate a sawtooth waveform of **frequency 1 kHz** with a UJT having $\eta = 0.7$, $V_{BB} = 20$ V, $V_D = 0.7$ V, and $V_V = 2$ V. Choose a suitable capacitor value and calculate the timing resistor. Also state the range of R for sustained oscillation given $I_P = 5$ µA and $I_V = 10$ mA.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Charging equation (2 marks):** With the UJT OFF, the capacitor charges via R from VBB. By KVL and the first-order ODE solution:

$$
V_{C}(t) \;=\; V_{BB} \left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Time to reach peak voltage (2 marks):** Setting $V_C(T_1) = V_P$:

$$
T_{1} \;=\; R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge time (1 mark):** Rapid discharge through the UJT:

$$
T_{2} \;=\; R_{d} C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{P}}{V_{V}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Total time period and conditions (2 marks):**

$$
T \;=\; R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)
$$

For sustained oscillation:

$$
R_{min} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{I_{V}} \qquad\qquad R_{max} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{I_{P}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{I_{V}} \;<\; R \;<\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{I_{P}}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute Peak Voltage (1 mark):**

$$
V_{P} \;=\; \eta V_{BB} + V_{D} \;=\; (0.7)(20) + 0.7 \;=\; 14 + 0.7 \;=\; 14.7 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Logarithmic Term (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}} \;=\; \frac{20 - 2}{20 - 14.7} \;=\; \frac{18}{5.3} \;\approx\; 3.396
$$

$$
\ln(3.396) \;\approx\; 1.223
$$

**Step 3 — Choose C and Calculate R (3 marks):** Let us select a convenient capacitor $C = 0.01$ µF (10 nF). Since $T = 1/f = 1/1000 = 1$ ms:

$$
R \;=\; \frac{T}{C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.01 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.223}
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{10^{-3}}{1.223 \times 10^{-8}} \;\approx\; 81.8 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

A standard **82 kΩ** resistor is chosen.

**Step 4 — Sustained Oscillation Range (2 marks):**

$$
R_{min} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{I_{V}} \;=\; \frac{20 - 2}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{18}{0.01} \;=\; 1800 \text{ }\Omega \;=\; 1.8 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{max} \;=\; \frac{V_{BB} - V_{P}}{I_{P}} \;=\; \frac{20 - 14.7}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{5.3}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 1.06 \text{ M}\Omega
$$

**Verification:** $1.8 \text{ k}\Omega < 82 \text{ k}\Omega < 1.06 \text{ M}\Omega$ ✓ — sustained oscillation guaranteed.

**Final Design Table:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|:----------|:------:|:------|
| Peak Voltage | $V_P$ | **14.7 V** |
| Frequency | $f$ | **1 kHz** |
| Time Period | $T$ | **1 ms** |
| Timing Capacitor | $C$ | **0.01 µF (10 nF)** |
| Timing Resistor | $R$ | **82 kΩ (standard)** |
| $R_{min}$ | — | **1.8 kΩ** |
| $R_{max}$ | — | **1.06 MΩ** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting V_D:** Many students compute $V_P = \eta V_{BB}$ alone, missing the **+0.7 V diode drop**. This single omission can cost **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the logarithm direction:** The natural log is $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB}}{V_{BB} - V_P}\right)$ for charging alone, and $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB} - V_V}{V_{BB} - V_P}\right)$ for the *combined* period. Mixing them up gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Ignoring the sustained-oscillation constraint:** In design problems, always verify $R_{min} < R < R_{max}$. Choosing R outside this range is a **3-mark deduction trap**.
> 4. **Misnaming terminals:** B1 and B2 are often swapped in diagrams. B1 is connected to the **lower potential (ground side)** and B2 to the **higher potential (VBB side)** — the **opposite** of an ordinary BJT.
> 5. **Conflating waveforms:** Students frequently mislabel the sawtooth at the **emitter** and the pulse at **RB1**. Examiner expects explicit **labelled axes** with units (V vs t).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **UJT = Unijunction Transistor** with one PN junction and three terminals (E, B1, B2); B1 is the lower-potential base.
- **Intrinsic Standoff Ratio:** $\eta = R_{B1}/R_{BB}$, typically **0.51 to 0.82** (e.g., 2N2646 → 0.65).
- **Peak Voltage Formula:** $V_{P} = \eta V_{BB} + V_{D}$, with $V_{D} \approx 0.6$ to 0.7 V.
- **Valley Voltage** $V_V$ and **Valley Current** $I_V$ are read from the manufacturer's datasheet.
- **Time Period:** $T = R C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{BB} - V_{V}}{V_{BB} - V_{P}}\right)$ — *memorise this equation verbatim*.
- **Frequency:** $f = 1/T$ — direct inverse.
- **Two outputs:** sawtooth at the **emitter (capacitor)**, sharp positive spike at **RB1**.
- **Sustained oscillation requires** $R_{min} < R < R_{max}$ where $R_{min} = (V_{BB}-V_V)/I_V$ and $R_{max} = (V_{BB}-V_P)/I_P$.
- **Relaxation oscillator** is used in medical electronics for **SCR triggering**, **defibrillator synchronisation**, and **time-base generation**.
- **RC product** sets the *speed* of oscillation: larger R or C → slower oscillation, and vice versa.
- **Always include V_D** in $V_P$ calculation — most-common KTU error.
- **UJT has negative resistance** between peak and valley points — this is the *heart* of the relaxation mechanism.
- **Frequency range** achievable: sub-Hz (with large C) to several hundred kHz (with small C and high-quality UJT).
- **Medical relevance:** UJT relaxation oscillators appear in **pacemaker timing circuits** and **patient-monitoring SCR triggers** in the KTU 2024 PCEBL507 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
