# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Thermistor** (Thermal Resistor) is a passive, solid-state, two-terminal semiconductor device whose electrical resistance is a strong, predictable, and nonlinear function of its absolute body temperature. In the context of KTU's Medical Electronics Lab, the thermistor is the foundational transducer used to convert the physiological/biomedical parameter of *temperature* into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage or current), making it indispensable in patient monitoring systems, neonatal incubators, digital thermometers, and respiration rate sensors.

Two dominant classes exist:
- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance **decreases** as temperature rises. This is the type universally used in clinical thermometry.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)** — Resistance **increases** with temperature; commonly used as inrush current limiters and motor protection devices.

The three characteristics studied in this experiment are:
- **V–I Characteristic** — Relationship between voltage across and current through the thermistor at a *constant* ambient temperature.
- **R–T Characteristic** — Resistance of the thermistor plotted against temperature (K or °C).
- **V–T Characteristic** — Output voltage of a *conditioning circuit* (voltage divider) plotted against the thermistor's body temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL507 – Module 2):**
> The lab demands **design + setup + observation + plotting** of all three characteristics. Exam questions test the student's ability to *justify circuit component selection*, *compute expected output voltage*, and *interpret nonlinear curves* (with the β constant and exponential model).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose connected to a municipal water tap**.

- When the tap valve is **tightened** (cold temperature analogy), the pipe opening becomes narrow, restricting water flow → this is *high resistance* (the NTC thermistor is "closed" to current at low T).
- When the tap valve is **loosened** (hot temperature analogy), the pipe widens, allowing more water through → this is *low resistance* (the NTC thermistor "opens" to current at high T).

Now think of the *water tap knob* as **thermal energy** (heat) and the *hose* as the **semiconductor lattice** of the thermistor. As we add heat (raise T), more charge carriers are released in the semiconductor → resistance drops.

A second analogy: the **NTC thermistor behaves like a temperature-controlled faucet**, while the **PTC behaves like a thermal fuse wire** that stiffens as it heats up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A thermistor is *not* a linear device. Unlike a standard resistor (R = ρL/A), its resistance changes by **3–5 % per °C** near room temperature and can change by **several orders of magnitude** over a 100 °C range. This is exactly why biomedical designers love it — high sensitivity to tiny temperature variations (e.g., 0.1 °C fever detection).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **β (Beta constant)** of a typical NTC thermistor: **3000 K – 5000 K** (medical-grade: ~3950 K)
- **Nominal resistance R₂₅**: Resistance at 25 °C, commonly **10 kΩ** in biomedical probes
- **Operating range (medical)**: 32 °C – 42 °C (clinical surface/skin temp)
- **Dissipation constant δ**: ~1 mW/°C (in still air)
- **Thermal time constant τ**: ~5 – 30 s for bead-type probes
- **Absolute zero**: **0 K = −273.15 °C** (reference for all thermistor math)
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The β-Equation (Steinhart-Hart Simplified Form)

The resistance of an NTC thermistor as a function of absolute temperature is governed by an exponential law derived from semiconductor charge-carrier statistics:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

where:

- **R_T** = resistance of the thermistor at absolute temperature **T** (in Kelvin)
- **R_0** = resistance at the reference temperature **T_0** (usually 298.15 K = 25 °C, so **R_0 = R₂₅**)
- **β** = material constant of the thermistor (in Kelvin), determined by the dopant concentration and lattice structure
- **e** = Euler's number ≈ **2.71828**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the relationship exponential and not linear?**
> In a semiconductor, the number of free charge carriers *n* follows the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution: *n* ∝ e^(−E_g / 2kT), where **E_g** is the band-gap energy and **k** is Boltzmann's constant. Conductivity is proportional to *n*, and **R ∝ 1/n**, hence R ∝ e^(+E_g / 2kT) ∝ e^(β/T).

## 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Mechanism

### Mechanism of V–I Characteristic (at constant T)

1. Apply a slowly varying DC voltage across the thermistor in series with an ammeter.
2. A voltmeter measures V across the thermistor.
3. At *low voltages*, the device obeys **Ohm's law** → V–I graph is a **straight line** with slope = R_T.
4. As current increases, **I²R power dissipation** heats the thermistor above ambient → R_T drops → V–I curve **bends downward** (nonlinear region).
5. The current at which deviation begins defines the **maximum safe operating current** before self-heating dominates.

### Mechanism of R–T and V–T Characteristics

1. The thermistor is immersed in a temperature-controlled water bath (or placed on a heated plate with a calibrated reference thermometer).
2. For R–T: a digital multimeter directly reads resistance at each temperature setting.
3. For V–T: the thermistor forms one arm of a **voltage divider** with a fixed series resistor R_S. The output is:
   - $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_T + R_S}$
4. As T increases → R_T decreases → V_out decreases (for NTC).
5. Plot V_out vs. T to obtain a *nonlinear, monotonically decreasing* curve.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta (1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Exponential β-model for NTC resistance | Ω |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)$ | Inverse form: temperature from resistance | K |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_T}{R_T + R_S}$ | Voltage divider output (NTC low-side) | V |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_S}{R_T + R_S}$ | Voltage divider output (NTC high-side) | V |
| 5 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{1}{R_T} \cdot \dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = -\dfrac{\beta}{T^2}$ | Temperature coefficient of resistance | K⁻¹ |
| 6 | $P = I^2 \cdot R_T = V \cdot I$ | Power dissipation causing self-heating | W |
| 7 | $T_{self} = T_{amb} + \dfrac{P}{\delta}$ | Self-heated steady-state temperature | K or °C |
| 8 | $\Delta T = T - 273.15$ | Conversion from Kelvin to Celsius | °C |
| 9 | $S = \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dT}$ | Circuit sensitivity (gain) | V/K |
| 10 | $S = -V_{in} \cdot R_S \cdot R_T \cdot \dfrac{\beta}{(R_T + R_S)^2 \cdot T^2}$ | Sensitivity in low-side NTC divider | V/K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Notice that the **sensitivity S** of a simple voltage divider is *not* constant — it is itself a function of T. KTU questions often ask the student to find the *temperature at which sensitivity is maximum*. Setting dS/dT = 0 yields the condition **R_T = R_S** at the desired operating point. This is the **matched-resistor design rule** for linearizing the divider around a chosen temperature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Digital Thermometers:** A 10 kΩ NTC bead at the probe tip, conditioned by a divider feeding an ADC. The MCU applies the β-equation in firmware.
- **Neonatal Incubator Air-Temperature Control:** Multiple NTC thermistors (air, skin, mattress) feed a PID controller to maintain 36.5 °C ± 0.3 °C.
- **Blood/Fluid Warming in Dialysis:** NTC placed in the line protects against overheating of patient fluids.
- **Respiratory Flow Sensors (pneumotach):** A self-heated NTC (constant-current mode) detects cooling by inspired air — used in spirometers.
- **MRI-Compatible Probes:** Special non-magnetic NTCs for patient monitoring inside the MRI bore.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the β-Equation from First Principles

### Step 1 — Start from semiconductor carrier statistics

The intrinsic carrier concentration in a semiconductor is given by:

$$n_i \propto T^{3/2} \cdot e^{-E_g / 2kT}$$

For an NTC thermistor (an extrinsic doped semiconductor), the resistance is inversely proportional to the free carrier density:

$$R_T \propto \frac{1}{n_i}$$

### Step 2 — Isolate the temperature-dependent term

Dropping the slowly varying $T^{3/2}$ term (a fair simplification in the medical temperature window 30 °C – 50 °C, where the variation is < 4 %):

$$R_T \approx R_0 \cdot e^{E_g / 2kT}$$

Comparing with the β-form, we identify the **β constant** as:

$$\beta = \frac{E_g}{2k}$$

For typical NTC oxides, **E_g ≈ 0.5 eV** and **k = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K**, giving **β ≈ 2900 K**, in excellent agreement with commercial thermistors (3000–5000 K, depending on dopant).

### Step 3 — Re-anchor to a known reference point

Since the constant of proportionality is unknown, we evaluate at T = T_0 = 298.15 K (25 °C):

$$R_0 = R_0 \cdot e^{E_g / 2k T_0}$$

Dividing the general expression by the reference expression eliminates the proportionality constant:

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{e^{E_g / 2kT}}{e^{E_g / 2kT_0}} = e^{\frac{E_g}{2k}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Final β-equation

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Exhaustive, KTU-Model Style)

**Given:**
- R₂₅ = **R_0 = 10 kΩ** at T_0 = **298.15 K**
- β = **3950 K**
- Series resistor in voltage divider: **R_S = 10 kΩ**
- Supply voltage: **V_in = 5 V**
- Compute V_out, sensitivity S, and the power dissipation for body temperature **T = 310.15 K (37 °C)**.

### Step 5 — Compute R_T at 37 °C

Using $T = 310.15$ K, $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $\beta = 3950$:

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{310.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$= 0.003224 - 0.003354 = -0.000130 \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{3950 \times (-0.000130)}$$

$$= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.5135}$$

$$= 10{,}000 \cdot 0.5983$$

$$= \mathbf{5983 \; \Omega \; \approx 5.98 \; k\Omega}$$

### Step 6 — Compute V_out

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_T}{R_T + R_S} = 5 \cdot \frac{5983}{5983 + 10000}$$

$$= 5 \cdot \frac{5983}{15983} = 5 \cdot 0.3743 = \mathbf{1.872 \; V}$$

### Step 7 — Compute the temperature coefficient of resistance α

$$\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2} = -\frac{3950}{(310.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{96193} = \mathbf{-0.04106 \; K^{-1}}$$

This means the resistance *decreases* by **4.1 % per Kelvin** of temperature rise around 37 °C.

### Step 8 — Compute circuit sensitivity S

$$S = -V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_S \cdot R_T}{(R_T + R_S)^2} \cdot \frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

$$S = -5 \cdot \frac{10000 \cdot 5983}{(15983)^2} \cdot \frac{3950}{(310.15)^2}$$

$$S = -5 \cdot \frac{5.983 \times 10^7}{2.5546 \times 10^8} \cdot 0.04106$$

$$S = -5 \cdot 0.2342 \cdot 0.04106$$

$$S = \mathbf{-0.0481 \; V/K}$$

Negative sign = V_out *decreases* with T (as expected for NTC low-side). The magnitude **48.1 mV/°C** is the usable sensitivity.

### Step 9 — Compute power dissipation and self-heating check

Current through divider:

$$I = \frac{V_{in}}{R_T + R_S} = \frac{5}{15983} = 0.313 \; \text{mA}$$

Power dissipated *in the thermistor only*:

$$P = I^2 \cdot R_T = (0.313 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 5983 = 5.86 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{W} = 0.586 \; \text{mW}$$

Self-heating temperature rise (assuming δ = 1 mW/°C in still air):

$$\Delta T_{self} = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{0.586}{1} = 0.586 \; °C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A self-heating error of **0.586 °C** in a clinical thermometer is **clinically unacceptable** (fever threshold is 0.5 °C above normal). KTU examiners love to ask: *"Is your circuit design safe for medical use? If not, suggest two modifications."* Answer: (1) Use a *pulsed excitation* (measure, then sleep), and (2) Increase R_S so that the current is < 100 μA, reducing P to < 0.1 mW.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Design Selection Table for R_S

| **Design Goal** | **Choose R_S** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum sensitivity at T_design = T_0 (25 °C) | R_S = R_0 = 10 kΩ | Maximum sensitivity occurs when R_T = R_S |
| Linearization around 37 °C (clinical) | R_S = R_37 ≈ 5.98 kΩ | Shifts the *peak sensitivity* to body temperature |
| Minimum self-heating (pediatric) | R_S ≈ 100 kΩ | Current drops to ~50 μA, P < 0.1 mW |
| Wheatstone bridge use | R_S matched to thermistor at midpoint | Maximizes bridge balance resolution |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Experiment Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[DC Regulated Power Supply 0 to 15 V] --> nodeB[Series Resistor RS 10 kOhm]
    nodeB --> nodeC[NTC Thermistor R_T 10 kOhm at 25 C]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Digital Voltmeter DV]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Digital Ammeter DA]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Common Ground GND]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Data Acquisition and Plotting]
    nodeH[Temperature Controlled Water Bath 30 to 60 C] --> nodeC
    nodeI[Mercury or Digital Reference Thermometer 0.1 C] --> nodeH
    nodeJ[Personal Computer with DAQ Software] --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeK[Graph 1 V versus I at constant T]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Graph 2 R versus T R T curve]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Graph 3 V_out versus T V T curve]
```

> The flow above shows the complete experimental chain: a regulated DC source feeds a series resistor RS, which forms a divider with the NTC thermistor RT immersed in a temperature-controlled bath. A reference thermometer (mercury or digital, ±0.1 °C) provides the calibration truth. The voltmeter reads V_out and the ammeter reads the loop current. Data is logged to a PC for plotting.

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic (Voltage Divider for V–T and R–T)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[V_in 5V DC] --> node2[Node A Top of Divider]
    node2 --> node3[Resistor R_S 10 kOhm]
    node3 --> node4[Node B Output Node]
    node2 --> node4
    node4 --> node5[Thermistor R_T NTC 10 kOhm 25 C]
    node5 --> node6[Ground 0V]
    node4 --> node7[Voltmeter V reads V_out]
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- Node A is held at +5 V.
- R_S is the upper (fixed) resistor.
- R_T is the lower (temperature-dependent) resistor — a true NTC.
- V_out is measured at Node B with respect to ground.
- For NTC: as T ↑, R_T ↓, so V_out ↓ (negative-slope transfer function).

## 4.3 Flowchart: Experimental Procedure Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Identify R_0 and beta from thermistor datasheet] --> step2[Step 2 Build voltage divider on breadboard R_S and R_T]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Set bath to 30 C and allow 5 min for thermal equilibrium]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Record V_out and I at constant T]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Increase bath in 5 C steps up to 60 C]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 At each step record T reference V_out and I]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7 Compute R_T equals V_out divided by I for each T]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8 Plot V I at fixed T 30 C and 50 C two curves]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9 Plot R versus T and V_out versus T]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10 Fit R T curve to beta equation and extract beta experimental]
```

## 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge Alternative (for Higher-Precision Measurements)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Excitation V_ex 2V DC] --> n1[Node A]
    n1 --> r1[R1 Fixed 10 kOhm]
    n1 --> r2[R2 Fixed 10 kOhm]
    r1 --> n2[Node B]
    r2 --> n3[Node C]
    n2 --> rt[Thermistor R_T]
    n3 --> r3[R3 Fixed 10 kOhm]
    rt --> gnd1[Ground]
    r3 --> gnd2[Ground]
    n2 --> galv[Differential Amplifier or Galvanometer V_diff]
    n3 --> galv
```

> When R_T = R3 (at T = T_design), the bridge is balanced and V_diff = 0. Small deviations from T_design produce a differential voltage amplified by an instrumentation stage. This is the topology used in **precision patient temperature monitors** and **fever-detection alarm circuits**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 marks]**
> **Q1.** Define a thermistor. Distinguish between NTC and PTC types with biomedical examples.

**Model Answer:**
A thermistor is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor resistor whose resistance varies predictably with temperature. An **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient)** thermistor shows a *decrease* in resistance as temperature rises; it is used in **clinical thermometers, incubators, and respiration sensors**. A **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient)** thermistor shows an *increase* in resistance with temperature and is used as a **self-resetting overcurrent protector** in biomedical equipment power supplies. (3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for NTC + 1 for PTC examples)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 marks]**
> **Q2.** State the β-equation of an NTC thermistor. Define each term and indicate its unit.

**Model Answer:**
The β-equation is:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

- **R_T** = resistance at temperature **T** (Ω)
- **R_0** = resistance at the reference temperature **T_0 = 25 °C = 298.15 K** (Ω)
- **β** = material constant characteristic of the thermistor (K)
- **T, T_0** = absolute temperatures in Kelvin (K)

(3 marks: 1 for the equation, 1 for variable definitions, 1 for correct units)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Set, 14 marks, Module 2]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *Voltage Divider Design & V–T Curve*

**(a)** Design a voltage-divider circuit to measure body temperature (32 °C – 42 °C) using a 10 kΩ NTC thermistor with β = 3950 K. Choose an appropriate series resistor R_S and supply voltage V_in such that the sensitivity at 37 °C is maximized. Justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute and tabulate V_out at T = 32 °C, 35 °C, 37 °C, 39 °C, and 42 °C. Plot the expected V–T curve and compute the circuit sensitivity at each temperature. Comment on linearity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Design choice — matched resistor rule:**
For maximum sensitivity at the design temperature, we require **R_T = R_S** at T = 37 °C = **310.15 K**.

$$R_{37} = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/310.15 - 1/298.15)}$$

$$\frac{1}{310.15} = 0.003224 \; \text{K}^{-1}, \quad \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.003354 \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$R_{37} = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{3950 \times (-0.000130)} = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.5135} = 10{,}000 \times 0.5983$$

$$\boxed{R_{37} \approx 5.98 \; \text{k}\Omega}$$

Therefore we select **R_S = 5.6 kΩ** (nearest E12 standard value) and **V_in = 5 V** (typical TTL/CMOS-compatible supply).

**Justification:**
- Matched-resistor condition shifts peak sensitivity into the clinical range.
- V_in = 5 V is standard for ADC interfacing.
- Low current (≈ 0.5 mA) limits self-heating to ≈ 0.6 °C — acceptable for non-critical monitoring.

#### Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Table of computed values:**

| **T (°C)** | **T (K)** | **R_T (Ω)** | **V_out (V)** | **Sensitivity S (mV/°C)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 32 | 305.15 | 7305 | 2.648 | -56.4 |
| 35 | 308.15 | 6464 | 2.460 | -52.1 |
| 37 | 310.15 | 5983 | 2.339 | -49.4 |
| 39 | 312.15 | 5536 | 2.221 | -46.7 |
| 42 | 315.15 | 4917 | 2.056 | -42.7 |

**Sample calculation for T = 32 °C:**

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{3950 (1/305.15 - 1/298.15)} = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.3135} = 7305 \; \Omega$$

$$V_{out} = 5 \times \frac{7305}{7305 + 5600} = 5 \times \frac{7305}{12905} = 2.648 \; \text{V}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$S = -V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_S R_T}{(R_S + R_T)^2} \cdot \frac{\beta}{T^2} = -5 \cdot \frac{5600 \cdot 7305}{(12905)^2} \cdot \frac{3950}{(305.15)^2}$$

$$S = -5 \cdot 0.3068 \cdot 0.04243 = -65.1 \; \text{mV/K} \approx -56.4 \; \text{mV/°C}$$

> **Comment on linearity:** The V–T curve is **monotonically decreasing and nonlinear** (concave upward). The sensitivity itself varies by ~25 % across the 10 °C clinical range. For high-precision applications, KTU recommends a **piecewise linear interpolation** in firmware, or a **lookup-table (LUT) calibration** using a calibrated reference thermometer at multiple points.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Self-Heating, V–I Curve, and Medical Safety*

**(a)** Derive the V–I characteristic of an NTC thermistor in still air, starting from the heat-balance equation. Show that the V–I curve is linear at low currents and bends due to self-heating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 10 kΩ NTC with β = 3950 K in a 5 V circuit through a 1 mA current, compute: (i) the self-heating temperature rise, (ii) the error in measured body temperature, and (iii) propose two engineering solutions to reduce this error. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Heat-balance equation:**
At thermal equilibrium, electrical power dissipated equals heat lost to the surroundings:

$$P_{elec} = P_{diss} \Rightarrow V \cdot I = \delta \cdot (T_{self} - T_{amb})$$

where **δ** is the dissipation constant (W/°C).

**Step 1:** At low currents, the temperature rise is negligible: T_self ≈ T_amb, so R_T ≈ R_0 (constant). Ohm's law gives:

$$V = R_0 \cdot I \quad \text{(linear region)}$$

**Step 2:** As I increases, T_self rises, R_T falls exponentially. The new operating point is found by simultaneously solving:

$$V = I \cdot R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left(\frac{1}{T_{amb} + P/\delta} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

$$V = I \cdot R_T(T_{self})$$

**Step 3:** Eliminating R_T by recognizing that the system finds a self-consistent (V, I) pair on a single curve, we get a **sub-linear V–I characteristic** — the curve *bends downward* at high currents because R_T shrinks faster than V can grow.

**Graphical output:** Linear from origin up to ~I = 0.1 mA, then curvature sets in; at very high currents, the curve may even become **negative-slope (NDR region)**.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b)

**(i) Self-heating temperature rise:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 5 \cdot 0.001 = 5 \; \text{mW}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \; °C$$

The thermistor self-heats by **5 °C above ambient** — a massive error in a clinical context.

**(ii) Error in measured body temperature:**
Assume the patient is at 37 °C and the room is also 25 °C. With 5 mW dissipation, the thermistor reads 25 + 5 = **30 °C** — a **7 °C error**, completely unacceptable.

**(iii) Two engineering solutions:**

1. **Pulsed excitation:** Drive the divider with a 1 % duty-cycle 5 V pulse (1 ms on, 99 ms off). Average power drops by 100× to 50 μW, so ΔT_self < 0.05 °C. Sample the ADC in the middle of the 'on' pulse.
2. **Increase series resistance:** Replace R_S = 10 kΩ with R_S = 100 kΩ. Loop current drops to ~50 μA, power to ~25 μW, self-heating to ~0.025 °C — safely below clinical resolution.
3. **(Bonus)** Use a **bridge topology** with auto-zeroing, which cancels DC offsets and allows AC excitation at low duty cycle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this experiment (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the β-equation — direct substitution of 37 in place of 310.15 is the #1 mistake. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Ignoring self-heating** in the answer — KTU expects a self-heating check *every time* a current flows through a thermistor. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Not specifying units** of β (Kelvin) and R_T (Ohm). (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Plotting V–I for multiple T values on the *same* graph** without a proper legend — examiner deducts for unclear presentation. (-0.5 mark)
> 5. **Missing the "justify R_S" sub-question** in design questions — a common 7-mark question is essentially asking for the *matched-resistor argument*. Skipping the justification loses 4 of 7 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the Steinhart-Hart 3-coefficient model** as a *more accurate* alternative — KTU's 2024 syllabus expects awareness of higher-order models.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize before the exam):**

- 🔹 **Thermistor = Thermal Resistor**, two main classes: **NTC** (used in medicine) and **PTC** (used as protection).
- 🔹 The **β-equation** is the master equation: $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ — *always use Kelvin*.
- 🔹 The **temperature coefficient α = −β/T²** is **negative** for NTC.
- 🔹 **V–I curve** at constant T is *linear at low I*, *bends downward* at high I due to **self-heating** ($\Delta T = P/\delta$).
- 🔹 **R–T curve** for NTC is a *steeply falling exponential* (semilog plot of R vs. 1/T is a straight line with slope = β·ln10).
- 🔹 **V–T curve** from a divider is *nonlinear and monotonically decreasing* (NTC low-side).
- 🔹 **Maximum divider sensitivity** occurs when **R_T = R_S** at the design temperature (matched-resistor design rule).
- 🔹 **Wheatstone bridge** is used for *high-precision* biomedical thermometry; the bridge output is *differential*.
- 🔹 **Self-heating** is the dominant error source; mitigate with **pulsed excitation** or **larger R_S**.
- 🔹 **Standard values**: R_0 = 10 kΩ, β = 3950 K, V_in = 5 V, R_S = 10 kΩ (textbook default).
- 🔹 **Steinhart-Hart equation** (3-coefficient) is the high-precision upgrade: $1/T = A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^3$.
- 🔹 **Medical applications** to mention in viva: digital thermometers, incubators, dialysis fluid warmers, respiration flow sensors, MRI-compatible probes.
- 🔹 **Plot conventions** for KTU lab record: V–I plotted for *at least two* temperature values on the same axes with a clear legend; R–T and V–T plotted with temperature on x-axis in °C (not K) for biomedical readability.
- 🔹 **Unit conversions**: K = °C + 273.15; never substitute °C directly into the β-equation.
- 🔹 **Steinhart-Hart vs β-model**: β-model has ~1 % error over a 50 °C range; Steinhart-Hart has ~0.01 % error over 100 °C — KTU bonus-credit content.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of LDR and Plot Characteristics (R vs Lux, V vs Lux)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL507 - MEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of LDR and Plot Characteristics (R vs Lux, V vs Lux)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)

A **Light Dependent Resistor (LDR)**, also called a **photoresistor** or **photo-conductive cell**, is a passive, two-terminal semiconductor device whose electrical resistance varies inversely with the intensity of incident light falling on its photosensitive surface. In the official KTU 2024 Scheme Medical Electronics syllabus, the LDR is classified as a **photo-conductive transducer** — a sensor that converts an optical input (luminous flux / illuminance) into a corresponding electrical output (resistance change).

The device exhibits its highest resistance in complete darkness (typically **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ**) and its lowest resistance under bright illumination (typically **few hundred ohms to a few kΩ**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEBL507 — Module 2)**
> The experiment is intended to demonstrate the *static characteristics* of an opto-electronic transducer by plotting **(a) Resistance vs Illumination (R vs Lux)** and **(b) Output Voltage vs Illumination (V vs Lux)** when interfaced through a **potential-divider (voltage-divider) network**.

## 1.2 Standard Illuminance Unit — The Lux

The input variable for the experiment is **illuminance (E)** measured in **lux (lx)**.
- **1 lux = 1 lumen per square metre (1 lx = 1 lm/m²)**
- Moonlight ≈ 0.1–1 lux, Office lighting ≈ 320–500 lux, Direct sunlight ≈ 32,000–100,000 lux.
- Human skin reflectance / pulse oximeter ambient context typically lies between **50 lx and 1000 lx**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tap** connected to a tank. When you open the tap fully, water flows easily (low resistance). When you close it partially, flow is restricted (high resistance).

An LDR works exactly like that, but instead of a hand turning the tap, **light photons do it**.

- In **darkness** → very few photons strike the semiconductor → the material behaves almost like an insulator → **Resistance is HIGH**.
- Under **bright light** → thousands of photons strike the surface → they knock electrons out of the valence band into the conduction band → the material becomes conductive → **Resistance is LOW**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Bright = Tight (low R), Dark = Loose (high R)"* — remember this and you will never confuse the LDR characteristic again in a KTU viva.

## 1.4 Construction Material & Physical Principle

| Parameter | Typical Value / Material |
|---|---|
| Active Material | **Cadmium Sulphide (CdS)** — most common |
| Alternate Materials | Cadmium Selenide (CdSe), Lead Sulphide (PbS) |
| Spectral Peak (CdS) | ~550 nm (matches human eye photopic response) |
| Response Time | ~20–100 ms (slow but adequate for medical/ambient sensing) |
| Operating Temperature | −30 °C to +70 °C |

> [!TIP]
> **Why CdS for Medical Electronics?** Its spectral response curve closely mirrors the photopic (daylight) response of the human eye (V<sub>λ</sub> curve), which makes it ideal for *ambient-light cancellation* in **pulse oximeters** and **photoplethysmography (PPG)** front-ends.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Photoconductive Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Photon Absorption:** When photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band-gap energy) strike the CdS surface, they are absorbed by valence-band electrons.
2. **Electron-Hole Pair Generation:** Each absorbed photon frees one electron, leaving a hole — creating an electron-hole pair.
3. **Conductivity Increase:** The free electrons reduce the effective resistance of the device. The conductivity $\sigma$ can be written as:

$$
\sigma = q \left( n_e \mu_e + n_h \mu_h \right)
$$

where $q$ is the electron charge, $n_e$ and $n_h$ are electron and hole carrier concentrations, and $\mu_e$, $\mu_h$ are their respective mobilities.

4. **Resistance Drop:** Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$, the resistance drops as the photon-generated carrier density rises.

5. **Steady-State Equilibrium:** After a few response-time constants, the recombination rate balances the generation rate and $R$ reaches a stable value for the prevailing illuminance.

## 2.2 The Empirical R-Lux Law (Hyperbolic Characteristic)

The R-Lux relationship is *non-linear* and is empirically modelled by the **power-law (Steinhart-like) equation**:

$$
\boxed{\,R(L) = A \cdot L^{-\alpha}\,}
$$

Where:
- $R(L)$ = LDR resistance at illuminance $L$ (in lux)
- $A$ = resistance constant when $L = 1$ lux (dark-region constant)
- $\alpha$ = sensitivity exponent (typically **0.7 to 0.9** for CdS)
- $L$ = illuminance in lux

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take the logarithm** of both sides to obtain a linear plot useful for graph paper work:

$$
\log R = \log A - \alpha \log L
$$

This is a straight line with slope $-\alpha$ on a **semi-log graph** of $\log R$ vs $\log L$. KTU examiners often ask students to verify the slope experimentally.

## 2.3 The Potential-Divider (Voltage Divider) Interface

To convert the resistance change into a measurable voltage, the LDR is connected in a **series voltage divider** with a fixed reference resistor $R_S$.

The output voltage tapped across $R_S$ (or across LDR, depending on configuration) is:

### Configuration 1 — Vout across R_S (most common for LDR)

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_S + R_{LDR}}
$$

### Configuration 2 — Vout across LDR

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_S + R_{LDR}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Trick:** In Configuration 1, as illumination increases → R_LDR decreases → Vout **increases** (direct/increasing characteristic). In Configuration 2, Vout **decreases** with light. Always draw and label the circuit before plotting.

## 2.4 Sensitivity

The system sensitivity (volts per lux) at any operating point is:

$$
S = \frac{dV_{out}}{dL} = -\frac{V_{CC} \, R_S}{(R_S + R_{LDR})^2} \cdot \frac{dR_{LDR}}{dL}
$$

Using $R_{LDR} = A L^{-\alpha}$:

$$
\frac{dR_{LDR}}{dL} = -A\alpha L^{-\alpha - 1}
$$

So the sensitivity magnitude is:

$$
\vert S \vert = \frac{V_{CC} \, R_S \, A \alpha \, L^{-\alpha - 1}}{(R_S + A L^{-\alpha})^2}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R(L) = A L^{-\alpha}$ | Empirical R-Lux power law | Ω |
| 2 | $\log R = \log A - \alpha \log L$ | Linear form for log-log plot | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_S / (R_S + R_{LDR})$ | Voltage divider across R_S | V |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{LDR} / (R_S + R_{LDR})$ | Voltage divider across LDR | V |
| 5 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{\Delta \log R}{\Delta \log L}$ | Slope of log-log graph (experimentally) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S = \vert dV_{out}/dL \vert$ | Static sensitivity at operating point | V/lx |
| 7 | $\tau \approx R_{LDR} \cdot C_{parasitic}$ | Time constant (response) | s |
| 8 | $E = \Phi / A_{surface}$ | Illuminance (luminous flux / area) | lx |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** Notice all absolute values are written as `\vert x \vert`, **not** `\vert x \vert`, to prevent the vertical pipe from breaking the markdown table layout. This is the KTU 2024 Scheme formatting standard expected in the answer script.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pulse Oximeters:** Ambient-light cancellation front-end (uses photodiode typically, but LDR can substitute in low-cost designs).
- **Automatic Room-Light Dimming:** Patient comfort in ICUs.
- **Sleep-Monitoring Wearables:** Detecting whether patient is in light or dark to correlate with melatonin / sleep quality.
- **Phototherapy Equipment:** Feedback loop for neonatal jaundice treatment lamps.
- **Smoke / Fire Alarms (medical wards):** Detecting sudden light-blockage events.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearisation of the R-Lux Power Law

We start with the empirical equation:

$$
R(L) = A L^{-\alpha}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply logarithm base 10 to both sides:**

$$
\log R(L) = \log (A L^{-\alpha})
$$

**Step 2 — Use the product rule of logarithms:**

$$
\log (A L^{-\alpha}) = \log A + \log L^{-\alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Bring the exponent in front:**

$$
\log R(L) = \log A - \alpha \log L
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the straight-line equation $y = mx + c$:**

- $y \equiv \log R$
- $m \equiv -\alpha$ (slope)
- $x \equiv \log L$
- $c \equiv \log A$ (y-intercept)

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Plotting $\log R$ on the y-axis against $\log L$ on the x-axis must yield a *straight line* of slope $-\alpha$ and intercept $\log A$. This is the **graphical method** to verify the power-law model from experimental data.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expression for $V_{out}$ vs $L$ (Closed Form)

We combine the voltage divider with the R-Lux power law.

**Step 1 — Voltage divider across R_S:**

$$
V_{out}(L) = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_S + R_{LDR}(L)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_{LDR}(L) = A L^{-\alpha}$:**

$$
V_{out}(L) = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_S + A L^{-\alpha}}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $L^{\alpha}$:**

$$
V_{out}(L) = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S \, L^{\alpha}}{R_S \, L^{\alpha} + A}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the half-saturation illuminance $L_0$ where $R_{LDR} = R_S$:**

From $A L_0^{-\alpha} = R_S$ we get $L_0 = \left(A / R_S\right)^{1/\alpha}$.

**Step 5 — Re-express $V_{out}$ in terms of $L_0$:**

$$
V_{out}(L) = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{(L / L_0)^{\alpha}}{1 + (L / L_0)^{\alpha}}
$$

This is a classic **sigmoidal (logistic-like) curve** that increases from 0 V (dark) to $V_{CC}$ (very bright).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Sensitivity at Operating Point

**Step 1 — Differentiate Step 2 of Derivation 2 w.r.t. $L$:**

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dL} = V_{CC} \, R_S \cdot \frac{d}{dL} \left( \frac{1}{R_S + A L^{-\alpha}} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply chain rule:**

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dL} = V_{CC} \, R_S \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{(R_S + A L^{-\alpha})^2} \right) \cdot \frac{d}{dL}(A L^{-\alpha})
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the power term:**

$$
\frac{d}{dL}(A L^{-\alpha}) = -A \alpha L^{-\alpha - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dL} = \frac{V_{CC} \, R_S \, A \, \alpha \, L^{-\alpha - 1}}{(R_S + A L^{-\alpha})^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Note the sign:**
Since $\alpha > 0$, the derivative is **positive** — $V_{out}$ rises monotonically with $L$ in Configuration 1. Sensitivity is **maximum near the half-saturation point $L = L_0$** and tends to zero at extreme dark or extreme bright regions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regimes

| Illuminance Range | LDR State | R_LDR Range | V_out (Config 1) | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Very Dark (0.1–10 lx) | Almost insulator | ~MΩ | ≈ 0 V | Very low |
| Indoor (100–500 lx) | Mid-range | ~kΩ | ≈ 0.3–0.7 V_CC | **Peak region** |
| Bright (1000–10,000 lx) | Conductive | ~hundreds of Ω | ≈ V_CC | Saturating |
| Direct Sun (>10,000 lx) | Highly conductive | < 100 Ω | ≈ V_CC | ≈ 0 |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- $V_{CC} = 5$ V
- $R_S = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$
- LDR parameters: $A = 5 \times 10^5$, $\alpha = 0.8$
- Illuminance $L = 500$ lux

**Step 1 — Compute R_LDR:**

$$
R_{LDR} = A L^{-\alpha} = 5 \times 10^5 \times (500)^{-0.8}
$$

We compute $(500)^{0.8}$:

$$
\log_{10}(500) = 2.69897
$$
$$
0.8 \times 2.69897 = 2.15918
$$
$$
500^{0.8} = 10^{2.15918} \approx 144.3
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{LDR} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{144.3} \approx 3465\ \Omega \approx 3.47\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute V_out:**

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,000 + 3465} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{13\,465} \approx 3.71\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity at this point:**

$$
S = \frac{5 \times 10\,000 \times 5 \times 10^5 \times 0.8 \times 500^{-1.8}}{(10\,000 + 3465)^2}
$$

Compute $(500)^{1.8} = 500 \times 500^{0.8} = 500 \times 144.3 = 72\,150$.

$$
S = \frac{5 \times 10^4 \times 4 \times 10^5 \times 0.8}{72\,150 \times (13\,465)^2}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{72\,150 \times 1.813 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{1.308 \times 10^{13}}
$$

$$
S \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V/lux} = 1.22\ \text{mV/lx}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This kind of substitution-and-evaluation problem is **frequently asked in KTU ESE Module 2 viva** — keep the steps clearly boxed in your answer book.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LDR Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[INCIDENT LIGHT PHOTONS] --> nodeB[CDS SEMICONDUCTOR FILM]
    nodeB --> nodeC[ELECTRON HOLE PAIR GENERATION]
    nodeC --> nodeD[CONDUCTIVITY INCREASE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[RESISTANCE DROP]
    nodeE --> nodeF[VOLTAGE DIVIDER NETWORK]
    nodeF --> nodeG[VOUT TO ADC OR METER]
    nodeG --> nodeH[MEDICAL INSTRUMENT FRONT END]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This block diagram maps the chain *optical input → material response → electrical output → instrument-level use*. It is the architecture expected in your KTU record-book block diagram.

## 4.2 Experimental Circuit Schematic (Voltage Divider with LDR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeVCC[VCC DC SUPPLY 5V] --> nodeN1[NODE A TOP]
    nodeN1 --> nodeLDR[LDR PHOTORESISTOR]
    nodeLDR --> nodeN2[NODE B MIDDLE]
    nodeN2 --> nodeRS[FIXED RESISTOR RS 10K]
    nodeRS --> nodeGND[GND 0V]
    nodeN2 --> nodeVOUT[VOUT TERMINAL TO DMM]
    subgraph MEASUREMENT_CHAIN [MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENTS]
        nodeDMM1[DMM1 ACROSS LDR MEASURES R]
        nodeDMM2[DMM2 AT NODE B MEASURES V]
        nodeLUX[LUX METER AT LDR FACE]
    end
    nodeVOUT -. MEASURED BY .-> nodeDMM2
    nodeLDR -. RESISTANCE READ BY .-> nodeDMM1
    nodeLDR -. EXPOSED TO .-> nodeLUX
```

> **Pro-Tip:** Always connect DMM-1 across the LDR alone to read resistance, and DMM-2 between node-B and ground to read Vout. This avoids loading errors.

## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY LDR TERMINALS AND DARK RESISTANCE] --> nodeS2[STEP 2 SET UP VOLTAGE DIVIDER ON BREADBOARD]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[STEP 3 POSITION LUX METER AT LDR FACE]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[STEP 4 SWITCH OFF ROOM LIGHT FULL DARK CONDITION]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[STEP 5 RECORD R AND VOUT FOR INCREASING LUX]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[STEP 6 INCREMENT LIGHT IN 10 STEPS]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[STEP 7 TABULATE OBSERVATIONS]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8[STEP 8 PLOT R VS LUX AND V VS LUX GRAPHS]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS9[STEP 9 VERIFY POWER LAW FROM LOG LOG PLOT]
```

## 4.4 Expected Graph Shapes (Schematic of Trends)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GRAPH_A [GRAPH A R VS LUX HYPERBOLIC]
        nodeA1[DARK HIGH R APPROX 1M] --> nodeA2[INDOOR MID R 10K]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[BRIGHT LOW R 500R]
    end
    subgraph GRAPH_B [GRAPH B V VS LUX SIGMOIDAL]
        nodeB1[DARK V APPROX 0V] --> nodeB2[INDOOR V APPROX 3V]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[BRIGHT V APPROX 5V]
    end
```

> **Important Drawing Convention:** The R-Lux curve is a *monotonically decreasing hyperbola*; the V-Lux curve is a *monotonically increasing sigmoid*. Always mark axes, units, and the operating-point marker on the record graph.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an LDR. Mention its active material and one medical-electronics application.**

**Model Answer:**
A Light Dependent Resistor (LDR) is a passive photo-conductive transducer whose resistance decreases with increasing incident light intensity. The active material is **Cadmium Sulphide (CdS)**, chosen because its spectral response peaks near 550 nm, matching the photopic response of the human eye. **Medical application:** It is used as an ambient-light sensor in the front-end of **pulse oximeters and photoplethysmography (PPG) probes** to compensate for varying room illumination.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Write the empirical R-Lux relationship for an LDR and explain the meaning of the exponent α.**

**Model Answer:**
The empirical relationship is $R(L) = A L^{-\alpha}$, where $A$ is the resistance at 1 lux and $\alpha$ is the sensitivity exponent. For CdS LDRs, $\alpha$ lies between **0.7 and 0.9**, indicating a strong inverse power-law dependence. Higher $\alpha$ implies greater sensitivity — small changes in light produce large resistance changes.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE allows internal choice between two questions of 14 marks. Solve **either** Question A or Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for output voltage of an LDR-based voltage divider. (7 marks)**

**(b) An LDR has $A = 2 \times 10^5$ and $\alpha = 0.85$. When connected in series with $R_S = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ across $V_{CC} = 5$ V, compute (i) the resistance at 200 lux, and (ii) the corresponding output voltage. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The LDR is connected in a potential divider with a fixed resistor $R_S$ across a DC source $V_{CC}$. The output is taken across $R_S$.

```
VCC ----+----[ LDR ]----+---- Vout ----+
                |          |            |
                |          +----[ RS ]--+
                |          |            |
GND ------------+----------+------------+
```

Applying the voltage-divider rule:

$$
V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_S + R_{LDR}}
$$

Substituting the empirical LDR law $R_{LDR} = A L^{-\alpha}$:

$$
V_{out}(L) = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_S}{R_S + A L^{-\alpha}}
$$

This is the required expression showing that $V_{out}$ rises monotonically as illuminance $L$ increases, reaching saturation at $V_{CC}$ for very high $L$.

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Compute R at L = 200 lux:**

$$
R_{LDR} = 2 \times 10^5 \times (200)^{-0.85}
$$

Compute $(200)^{0.85}$:

$$
\log_{10}(200) = 2.3010
$$
$$
0.85 \times 2.3010 = 1.9559
$$
$$
200^{0.85} = 10^{1.9559} \approx 90.36
$$

$$
R_{LDR} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{90.36} \approx 2213\ \Omega \approx 2.21\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compute V_out:**

$$
V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{10\,000 + 2213} = 5 \cdot \frac{10\,000}{12\,213}
$$

$$
V_{out} \approx 4.09\ \text{V}
$$

**Final answers:** $R_{LDR} \approx 2.21\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{out} \approx 4.09$ V.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the photoconductive principle of an LDR. List the constructional features and discuss why CdS is preferred for medical light sensing. (7 marks)**

**(b) Sketch the expected R-Lux and V-Lux characteristics. From the log-log plot of R vs L, derive the linearised equation and explain how the exponent α is obtained graphically. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Photoconductive Principle:**
When photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ fall on the CdS surface, they liberate electrons from the valence band into the conduction band, producing electron-hole pairs. This raises the carrier concentration $n_e, n_h$ and hence the conductivity:

$$
\sigma = q(n_e \mu_e + n_h \mu_h)
$$

Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$, the resistance of the device drops as light intensity rises.

**Constructional Features:**
- Thin CdS film deposited on a ceramic substrate.
- Two interdigitated electrodes to maximise exposed active area.
- Hermetically sealed transparent encapsulation.
- Two axial lead terminals.

**Why CdS for Medical Electronics:**
- Spectral response peak ≈ 550 nm, matching the **photopic luminosity curve V(λ)** of the human eye.
- Good sensitivity in the **500–700 lux** range typical of indoor clinical environments.
- Low cost, robust, and easily interfaced to standard op-amp front-ends for ambient-light cancellation in pulse oximeters and patient monitors.

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Sketch the graphs:**

```
R-Lux curve:   R│ •  (very high in dark)
                 │  •
                 │   ••
                 │     ••••
                 │         ••••••••••••    L
                 
V-Lux curve:   V│                •••••••  (saturates at Vcc)
                 │           ••••
                 │       •••
                 │     •
                 │   •
                 │ •            L
```

**Linearisation (log-log plot):**

Starting from $R(L) = A L^{-\alpha}$, take log of both sides:

$$
\log R = \log A - \alpha \log L
$$

This is the equation of a straight line $y = mx + c$ with:
- y = $\log R$
- slope $m = -\alpha$
- x-intercept and y-intercept give $A$ and the half-saturation point.

**Graphical Method to Find α:**

From the log-log plot, pick two well-separated points $(L_1, R_1)$ and $(L_2, R_2)$. Then:

$$
\alpha = - \frac{\log R_2 - \log R_1}{\log L_2 - \log L_1}
$$

The negative of this slope is the sensitivity exponent α. For CdS LDRs, the experimentally obtained α typically lies between **0.7 and 0.9**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the configurations** — students often write Vout across the LDR when the question clearly shows the output tapped across R_S. **Always redraw and label the circuit before writing the formula.**
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the linearised log-log plot — the slope is **negative**, not positive.
> 3. **No units on the graph axes** — KTU record-book evaluation explicitly deducts marks for missing lx on x-axis and Ω on y-axis.
> 4. **Mixing up resistance measurement and voltage measurement** — R-Lux requires an ohmmeter across the LDR alone; V-Lux requires the divider to be powered.
> 5. **Skipping the light-meter reading** — without lux values, the graphs cannot be plotted. Tabular columns must always be: Lux (lx) | R (kΩ) | Vout (V).
> 6. **Using a single linear scale** — R-Lux looks like a hyperbola on linear axes, but a straight line on log-log axes. Failing to mention this loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Not mentioning CdS spectral match to V(λ)** when explaining medical suitability — this is the favourite one-liner of KTU external examiners.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LDR** = Light Dependent Resistor = **photoresistor** = photo-conductive cell.
- **Material:** Cadmium Sulphide (CdS); peak sensitivity at **~550 nm**.
- **Behaviour:** Resistance **decreases** as illumination **increases** (inverse).
- **Dark resistance:** typically **1–10 MΩ**.
- **Bright resistance:** typically **few hundred ohms to a few kΩ**.
- **Empirical law:** $R(L) = A L^{-\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ for CdS.
- **Linearised form:** $\log R = \log A - \alpha \log L$ (straight line on log-log plot).
- **Voltage divider (across R_S):** $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_S / (R_S + R_{LDR})$ — **rises** with light.
- **Voltage divider (across LDR):** $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{LDR} / (R_S + R_{LDR})$ — **falls** with light.
- **Sensitivity peak:** occurs near the half-saturation point where $R_{LDR} = R_S$.
- **Time constant:** $\tau = R_{LDR} \times C_{parasitic}$ (slow device, ~20–100 ms).
- **Medical applications:** ambient-light sensing for pulse oximeters, ICU automatic lighting, phototherapy feedback, sleep-stage monitors.
- **Graph shapes to memorise:** R-Lux = **decreasing hyperbola**; V-Lux = **increasing sigmoid**.
- **Always label:** x-axis in **lux (lx)**, y-axis in **Ω** or **V** as appropriate.
- **Light source:** use a controlled variable-intensity lamp with a calibrated lux meter placed at the LDR face.
- **KTU Examiner Pet Question:** *"Why CdS and not silicon?"* — Answer: CdS spectral peak matches the photopic V(λ) eye-response curve, ideal for medical ambient-light compensation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Setup of Biotelemetry Circuit Using IC 4046 (Transmitting PCG/ECG Signals)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEBL507 - MEDICAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Setup of Biotelemetry Circuit Using IC 4046 (Transmitting PCG/ECG Signals)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biotelemetry — The Formal Definition

**Biotelemetry** is the science and technology of automatically measuring and transmitting biological (physiological) data from a remote or inaccessible location to a receiver where the data can be analysed, recorded, or displayed. In the KTU 2024 Medical Electronics Lab context, it refers to the wireless transmission of bioelectric signals — primarily **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** and **PCG (Phonocardiogram)** — from a patient to a monitoring station without any physical wired connection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment expects the student to *design* (not just *connect*) a complete Frequency Modulated (FM) transmitter subsystem in which the **IC CD4046 (Phase Locked Loop)** acts as the Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) whose instantaneous output frequency is directly proportional to the amplitude of the bioelectric input.

The standard bioelectric signal characteristics that drive the design are tabulated below:

| Signal | Frequency Band | Amplitude Range | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ECG** | 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz | 0.5 mV – 5 mV | Surface Ag/AgCl electrodes |
| **PCG** | 20 Hz – 2000 Hz | Variable (sound-derived) | Microphone / piezoelectric pickup |
| **EEG** | 0.5 Hz – 100 Hz | 10 µV – 200 µV | Scalp electrodes |
| **EMG** | 20 Hz – 5000 Hz | 50 µV – 5 mV | Surface / needle electrodes |

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — A "Radio Station Inside the Body"

Imagine a tiny radio station sitting on a patient's chest. The patient's heart is constantly producing faint electrical whispers (the ECG, about 1 mV). Without a biotelemetry circuit, that whisper is too weak and too low-frequency to "travel." What we do is this:

1. **Amplify the whisper** using a high-gain instrumentation amplifier.
2. **Convert the whisper into a frequency wobble** of a much higher "carrier wave" (typically in the **FM broadcast band of 88 MHz – 108 MHz** or the low-power **433 MHz ISM band**). This conversion is called **Frequency Modulation (FM)**.
3. **Transmit** the FM carrier via a small antenna.
4. **Receive** it at a remote station, then **decode** the wobble back into the original ECG waveform.

The **IC 4046** is the heart of step 2 — it is the device that performs the *whisper-to-wobble* translation using its built-in **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realisation:** When a low-frequency ECG voltage rises and falls (the P-QRS-T wave), the IC 4046's output frequency rises and falls in exact lockstep. The receiver measures these tiny frequency variations and converts them back to a voltage. The result is a clean replica of the ECG, transmitted wirelessly.

## 1.3 Why Frequency Modulation (and not Amplitude Modulation)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FM is preferred over AM in biotelemetry because:** (i) FM is highly immune to biological and environmental noise (powerline interference, muscle artefacts, electrostatic pickup), (ii) the information is carried in **frequency** rather than amplitude, and (iii) the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) improves by the square of the modulation index, providing superior diagnostic fidelity.

The complete experimental block — the one you will set up on the lab breadboard — therefore consists of a **pre-amplifier → LPF (anti-aliasing) → VCO (IC 4046) → RF stage → Antenna**, with a complementary **Receiver → PLL Demodulator → Output Amplifier** on the monitoring side.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of the IC CD4046 (The Heart of the Experiment)

The **CD4046** is a CMOS Micropower Phase-Locked Loop containing **two key sub-blocks** that we use independently for this experiment:

### Block A — The Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)
This is the block we use. It produces a square-wave output whose frequency is a linear function of the input DC voltage at pin 9. The VCO section is built around a current-starved inverter ring oscillator whose charging current is set by an external resistor **R1** connected from pin 11 to VDD, and an external capacitor **C1** connected from pin 6/7 to ground.

### Block B — The Phase Comparators (PC1, PC2)
We do **not** use these in the VCO-only experiment, but they are essential for the receiver side (where a second 4046 acts as a Phase Locked demodulator).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of the VCO Section

1. **Input voltage Vin** (the amplified bioelectric signal after offsetting) is applied to **pin 9** (the VCO input).
2. Internally, a voltage-to-current converter produces a charging current $I_c$ proportional to $V_{in} - V_{SS}$.
3. This current alternately charges and discharges the external capacitor $C_1$ between two threshold voltages ($V_{DD}/2 \pm V_{offset}$).
4. The charging time per half-cycle sets the oscillation period.
5. **Output frequency $f_{out}$** appears as a 50 % duty cycle square wave at **pin 4**.

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between $V_{in}$ and $f_{out}$ is highly linear in the central region, but saturates near the rails. The linear range is approximately **0.2 V to (VDD − 0.2 V)**. Therefore, the amplified ECG (which sits near 0 V with excursions of ±5 mV) MUST first be DC-offset to sit in the middle of this linear range (e.g., 2.5 V for a 5 V supply).

## 2.3 Critical Pin Functions of the 4046 (Pinout Reference)

| Pin No. | Symbol | Function |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | PCPout | Phase Comparator Pulse Output |
| 2 | PC1out | Phase Comparator 1 Output |
| 3 | COMPIN | Comparator Input |
| 4 | VCOout | **VCO Output (our FM carrier source)** |
| 5 | INH | Inhibit (HIGH = disable VCO) |
| 6, 7 | C1A, C1B | External Capacitor $C_1$ connection |
| 8 | VSS | Ground (0 V) |
| 9 | VCOin | **VCO Control Voltage Input (our ECG signal goes here)** |
| 10 | DEMout | Demodulator Output (used in receiver) |
| 11 | R1 | External Resistor $R_1$ connection |
| 12 | R2 | External Resistor $R_2$ (sets frequency offset) |
| 13 | PC2out | Phase Comparator 2 Output |
| 14 | SIGin | Signal Input for Phase Comparators |
| 15 | ZENER | Internal 5.2 V Zener cathode |
| 16 | VDD | Positive Supply (+5 V to +15 V) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the **mathematical core** of the experiment. Memorise these — at least three of them will appear in derivations and numerical problems in the university exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f_{center} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \, C_1}$ | VCO free-running (center) frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \, C_1}$ @ $V_{in} = 0$ | Lower frequency limit | Hz |
| 3 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \, C_1} \cdot \dfrac{V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_{offset}}$ | Upper saturation frequency | Hz |
| 4 | $K_0 = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{V_{DD}}$ | VCO Gain (Hz/V) — analogous to $K_f$ | Hz/V |
| 5 | $K_0 = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \, C_1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{V_{DD}}$ (ideal) | Simplified gain for small-signal analysis | Hz/V |
| 6 | $\Delta f = K_0 \cdot A_m$ | Frequency deviation produced by bio-signal of peak amplitude $A_m$ | Hz |
| 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ | FM Modulation Index (dimensionless) | — |
| 8 | $BW = 2 (\Delta f + f_{m,max})$ | Carson's Rule — 98 % power Bandwidth | Hz |
| 9 | $G_{pre} = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \, k\Omega}{R_G}$ | Instrumentation Amplifier Gain (INA128 family) | — |
| 10 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | First-order LPF Cutoff Frequency | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** Notice that the VCO center frequency is *inversely* proportional to both $R_1$ and $C_1$. To shift the carrier from 1 kHz (audio tone demonstration) to 100 kHz (practical biotelemetry), you must reduce the product $R_1 C_1$ by a factor of 100.

## 2.5 Design Reasoning — The "Why" Behind the Numbers

> **Why $f_{center} \approx 100$ kHz in lab demos?** — A pure audio frequency (e.g., 1 kHz tone) is too low to be observed on a standard oscilloscope as a clean FM sweep. Choosing 100 kHz puts the carrier in a range where you can clearly see the frequency wobble (a "fanning" of the oscilloscope trace) when a 1 Hz ECG simulator tone is injected.

> **Why a 3-stage design (pre-amp → LPF → VCO)?** — Bioelectric signals contain high-frequency noise (50 Hz powerline, RF interference). An LPF with $f_c \approx 150$ Hz for ECG (or $\approx 2$ kHz for PCG) prevents the VCO from being modulated by noise, which would otherwise produce a noisy FM carrier and degrade the received waveform.

> **Why use an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128, AD620)?** — ECG/PCG sources are *differential* and of very low amplitude (mV range). An INA provides (a) very high Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR ≥ 100 dB at 60 Hz), (b) high input impedance (≥ 10 GΩ), and (c) precise gain set by a single resistor.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Biotelemetry circuits built around the 4046 VCO principle are the **architectural grandparent of every modern wearable health device** — Holter monitors, wireless ECG patches (e.g., iRhythm Zio), Bluetooth ECG straps, and implantable pacemakers. Although modern devices use digital FSK/PSK modulators, the **fundamental principle remains identical**: convert a low-frequency bioelectric voltage into a proportional frequency shift of a high-frequency carrier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — VCO Center Frequency for the CD4046

The internal current mirror of the 4046 sources a charging current:

$$
I_c = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{offset}}{R_1}
$$

This current linearly charges $C_1$ through a Schmitt trigger inverter. The time taken to charge from the lower threshold ($V_{SS} = 0$ V) to the upper threshold ($V_{DD}/2$) is:

$$
T_{charge} = \frac{C_1 \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{I_c} = \frac{C_1 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot R_1}{2(V_{DD} - V_{offset})}
$$

Because the oscillator is symmetric, the discharge time is identical, giving a full period:

$$
T = 2 T_{charge} = \frac{C_1 \, V_{DD} \, R_1}{V_{DD} - V_{offset}}
$$

Inverting the period to obtain frequency:

$$
f_{center} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{V_{DD} - V_{offset}}{R_1 \, C_1 \, V_{DD}}
$$

For $V_{DD} \gg V_{offset}$ (which is approximately 0.5 V for the CD4046), the offset is often neglected, yielding the canonical textbook formula:

$$
\boxed{\,f_{center} \approx \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1}\,}
$$

> **Design Check:** Choose $R_1 = 10 \, k\Omega$ and $C_1 = 1 \, nF$.

$$
f_{center} = \frac{1}{(10 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}
$$

This 100 kHz carrier is ideal for a lab demonstration.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VCO Gain Constant $K_0$

The VCO gain (or modulation constant) is the slope of the $f_{out}$ versus $V_{in}$ transfer characteristic. Differentiating the general expression with respect to $V_{in}$:

$$
K_0 = \frac{df_{out}}{dV_{in}} = \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{DD} - V_{offset}}
$$

Substituting the design values from above with $V_{DD} = 5 \, V$ and $V_{offset} \approx 0.5 \, V$:

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{10^4 \times 10^{-9}} \cdot \frac{1}{5 - 0.5} = 10^5 \cdot \frac{1}{4.5}
$$

$$
K_0 = 22.22 \text{ kHz/V}
$$

This means **every 1 V change at the VCO input shifts the carrier by 22.22 kHz**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Deviation Produced by a 1 mV ECG

After the instrumentation amplifier (gain $G = 1000$) and the DC offset adder (adding 2.5 V to centre the ECG in the VCO linear range), a peak ECG amplitude of 1 mV becomes:

$$
V_{peak} = G \cdot A_m = 1000 \times 1 \text{ mV} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

The resulting frequency deviation is:

$$
\Delta f = K_0 \cdot V_{peak} = (22.22 \text{ kHz/V}) \times 1 \text{ V} = 22.22 \text{ kHz}
$$

The carrier therefore sweeps between:

$$
f_{min} = 100 \text{ kHz} - 22.22 \text{ kHz} = 77.78 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
f_{max} = 100 \text{ kHz} + 22.22 \text{ kHz} = 122.22 \text{ kHz}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Modulation Index (Dimensionless)

The modulation index is defined as the ratio of frequency deviation to the highest modulating frequency:

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_{m,max}}
$$

For an ECG with $f_{m,max} = 150$ Hz:

$$
\beta = \frac{22.22 \text{ kHz}}{150 \text{ Hz}} = 148.1
$$

This very high $\beta$ value means the system operates in the **Wideband FM (WBFM)** regime, which is desirable for noise immunity.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Transmission Bandwidth (Carson's Rule)

The bandwidth required to transmit an FM signal without distortion is given by Carson's Rule:

$$
BW = 2 (\Delta f + f_{m,max})
$$

Substituting the ECG values:

$$
BW = 2 (22.22 \text{ kHz} + 150 \text{ Hz}) = 2 \times 22.37 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,BW \approx 44.74 \text{ kHz}\,}
$$

This fits comfortably within the standard IF bandwidth of FM broadcast receivers (200 kHz) and lab audio receivers (50 kHz – 200 kHz).

## 3.6 Complete Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Standard)

> **Question:** Design a biotelemetry transmitter using IC 4046 to transmit an ECG signal of peak amplitude 2 mV and highest frequency 100 Hz. The carrier frequency must be 50 kHz and the pre-amplifier gain must be limited to 500. Use $V_{DD} = 10$ V. Determine (a) the values of $R_1$ and $C_1$, (b) the VCO gain $K_0$, (c) the frequency deviation, and (d) the modulation index.

### Step (a): Choose $C_1 = 1$ nF (standard value), then:

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{f_{center} \cdot C_1} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

### Step (b): VCO Gain

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1 \, (V_{DD} - V_{offset})} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.5} \approx 5.26 \text{ kHz/V}
$$

### Step (c): Peak amplified voltage entering VCO

$$
V_{peak} = G \cdot A_m = 500 \times 2 \text{ mV} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\Delta f = K_0 \cdot V_{peak} = 5.26 \text{ kHz}
$$

### Step (d): Modulation Index

$$
\beta = \frac{5260}{100} = 52.6
$$

**Answer Summary:** $R_1 = 20 \, k\Omega$, $C_1 = 1 \, nF$, $K_0 \approx 5.26 \text{ kHz/V}$, $\Delta f \approx 5.26 \text{ kHz}$, $\beta = 52.6$.

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose What

| Design Parameter | ECG Application | PCG Application | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f_{center}$ | 50 – 100 kHz | 200 – 500 kHz | PCG needs wider sweep |
| $f_{m,max}$ | 150 Hz | 2000 Hz | PCG has higher spectral content |
| LPF Cutoff | 150 Hz | 2000 Hz | Anti-aliasing / noise rejection |
| $C_1$ (with $R_1 = 10$ kΩ) | 1 nF → 100 kHz | 200 pF → 500 kHz | Frequency scaling |
| Modulation Index $\beta$ | > 50 (WBFM) | > 10 (WBFM) | High SNR requirement |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Biotelemetry System Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the full signal flow of the experiment — from the patient's body to the remote monitoring display. It captures both the **transmitter (Tx)** side and the **receiver (Rx)** side that together constitute a complete biotelemetry link.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIO["BIOLOGICAL SIGNAL SOURCE"]
        A1["ECG Electrodes\nAg/AgCl Surface Electrodes\nOutput: 0.5 mV to 5 mV, 0.05 to 150 Hz"]
        A2["PCG Microphone / Piezo Pickup\nOutput: variable amplitude, 20 Hz to 2 kHz"]
    end

    subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER STAGE - PATIENT SIDE"]
        B1["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER\nINA128 or AD620\nGain G = 1 + 49.4k/RG\nTypical G = 1000"]
        B2["DC OFFSET ADDER\nSumming Amplifier\nAdds 2.5V to centre VCO range"]
        B3["LOW PASS FILTER - LPF\nSecond Order Butterworth\nfc = 150 Hz for ECG\nfc = 2 kHz for PCG"]
        B4["VCO - IC CD4046 Pin 9\nCenter Frequency fc = 1 over R1 C1\nR1 = 10k, C1 = 1nF gives 100kHz"]
        B5["RF BUFFER and ANTENNA\nSquare Wave to Antenna\nRadiates FM signal"]
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL["WIRELESS CHANNEL"]
        C1["FREE SPACE or WIRE\n433 MHz ISM or 100 kHz Lab Carrier\nNoise and Attenuation added"]
    end

    subgraph RX["RECEIVER STAGE - MONITOR SIDE"]
        D1["RECEIVING ANTENNA and RF AMP\nRecovers FM Signal\nBandwidth = 50 kHz"]
        D2["PLL DEMODULATOR\nSecond IC 4046 or LM565\nConverts FM back to Voltage"]
        D3["LOW PASS RECONSTRUCTION FILTER\nfc same as Tx LPF\nRemoves carrier ripple"]
        D4["OUTPUT AMPLIFIER\nGain 10 to 50\nDrives CRO or Display"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["DISPLAY and RECORDING"]
        E1["OSCILLOSCOPE / PC / DSO\nObserved Waveform\nECG: P QRS T complex\nPCG: Lub Dub S1 S2 sounds"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of the CD4046 VCO Section

The diagram below zooms into the **internal workings of the 4046 VCO block** so that the student can clearly see how the external $R_1$ and $C_1$ interact with the on-chip current mirror and Schmitt inverter to generate the FM carrier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VCOINT["IC CD4046 INTERNAL VCO STRUCTURE"]
        V1["Pin 9 - VCO Input\nECG Signal + 2.5V offset"]
        V2["Voltage to Current Converter\nOn Chip Current Mirror"]
        V3["Pin 11 - R1 connection\nExternal Resistor 10 kohm"]
        V4["Pin 6 7 - C1 connection\nExternal Capacitor 1 nF"]
        V5["Schmitt Trigger Inverter\nThreshold VDD over 2"]
        V6["Pin 4 - VCO Output\nFM Square Wave to RF Stage"]
    end

    V1 --> V2
    V3 --> V2
    V2 -->|Charging current Ic| V4
    V4 --> V5
    V5 --> V6
    V5 -->|Feedback| V4
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow Timing — Frequency Wobble Visualisation

This final diagram shows the conceptual relationship between the **bioelectric voltage (Vin)** and the **resulting VCO output frequency (fout)**. It is the single most important concept to grasp for the KTU viva and the design question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WAVE1["INPUT Vin at Pin 9 - Bioelectric Signal"]
        W1["ECG P Wave +0.2 mV\nQRS Complex minus 1 mV\nT Wave plus 0.3 mV\nDC Offset = 2.5V"]
    end

    subgraph WAVE2["VCO OUTPUT FO relationship"]
        W2["Voltage to Frequency Transfer\nLinear Slope K0 = 22.2 kHz per V\nfout = 100 kHz plus or minus delta f"]
    end

    subgraph WAVE3["TRANSMITTED FM SIGNAL"]
        W3["Carrier at 100 kHz\nFrequency deviates up to 122.22 kHz\nFrequency deviates down to 77.78 kHz\nInstantaneous frequency tracks ECG"]
    end

    W1 -->|Modulation| W2
    W2 -->|FM Output| W3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define biotelemetry. List the two main functional blocks of an FM biotelemetry transmitter.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Biotelemetry** is the technique of measuring and transmitting biological signals from a remote or inaccessible source to a monitoring station via a wired or wireless link, allowing physiological parameters (ECG, PCG, EEG) to be observed without physical contact.
>
> The **two main functional blocks** of an FM biotelemetry transmitter are:
> 1. **Signal Conditioning Stage** — Instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) followed by a low-pass anti-aliasing filter.
> 2. **Modulation and Transmitter Stage** — Voltage Controlled Oscillator (IC 4046) which converts the bioelectric voltage into a frequency-modulated carrier, followed by an RF buffer and antenna.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the function of pin 9 of IC 4046? Why must the ECG signal be DC-offset before applying it to this pin?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Pin 9** of the IC CD4046 is the **VCO Control Voltage Input**. The instantaneous frequency of the VCO output (at pin 4) is a linear function of the DC voltage applied at this pin.
>
> The ECG signal (≈ 1 mV, bipolar) must be **DC-offset by approximately VDD/2** (i.e., 2.5 V for a 5 V supply) before being applied to pin 9 because the VCO's linear operating range is approximately 0.2 V to (VDD − 0.2 V). Without offsetting, the ECG would drive the VCO below its lower linear threshold, resulting in severe distortion (clipped, non-linear FM) and loss of the diagnostic QRS complex.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** KTU 2024 scheme Part B lab questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7**. You are given an internal choice — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]

**Design an FM biotelemetry transmitter to wirelessly transmit an ECG signal of peak amplitude 1.5 mV and frequency range 0.05 Hz to 120 Hz. The carrier frequency is to be 80 kHz, VDD = 10 V, and the pre-amplifier gain is 800. Use IC CD4046 as the VCO.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Determine the values of $R_1$ and $C_1$ for the 4046 VCO, and calculate the VCO gain constant $K_0$.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose a standard $C_1$:** A 1 nF (1000 pF) ceramic capacitor is the most stable and widely available value. So, $C_1 = 1 \times 10^{-9}$ F.

**Step 2 — Compute $R_1$ from the canonical VCO formula:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{f_{center} \cdot C_1} = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R_1 = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 12.5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Practical value:** Use $R_1 = 12 \, k\Omega$ (E12 standard) in series with a $1 \, k\Omega$ trimpot to allow fine-tuning to exactly 80 kHz.

**Step 4 — Compute $K_0$ with $V_{offset} \approx 0.5$ V (CD4046 datasheet value):**

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1 \, (V_{DD} - V_{offset})} = \frac{1}{12.5 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.5}
$$

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{1.1875 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 8.42 \text{ kHz/V}
$$

**Answer:** $R_1 = 12.5 \, k\Omega$ (use 12 kΩ + 1 kΩ trimpot), $C_1 = 1$ nF, $K_0 \approx 8.42$ kHz/V.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the peak amplified voltage, frequency deviation, modulation index, and Carson's bandwidth for the design.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Peak amplified voltage entering the VCO:**

$$
V_{peak} = G \cdot A_m = 800 \times 1.5 \text{ mV} = 1200 \text{ mV} = 1.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency deviation:**

$$
\Delta f = K_0 \cdot V_{peak} = 8.42 \text{ kHz/V} \times 1.2 \text{ V} = 10.1 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Modulation index** (using $f_{m,max} = 120$ Hz):

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_{m,max}} = \frac{10100}{120} \approx 84.2
$$

**Step 4 — Carson's Bandwidth:**

$$
BW = 2 (\Delta f + f_{m,max}) = 2 (10100 + 120) = 2 \times 10220
$$

$$
\boxed{BW = 20440 \text{ Hz} \approx 20.44 \text{ kHz}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system operates in the WBFM regime with $\beta = 84.2$, the carrier wobbles from approximately 70 kHz to 90 kHz, and the receiver IF must accommodate at least 21 kHz of bandwidth.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Explain the working of a biotelemetry circuit using IC 4046 to transmit a PCG signal. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of each stage. Given: $R_1 = 4.7$ kΩ, $C_1 = 1$ nF, $V_{DD} = 9$ V, peak PCG amplitude = 3 mV, pre-amp gain = 200, $f_{m,max} = 2$ kHz. Calculate the carrier frequency, $K_0$, $\Delta f$, and $\beta$.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of each block in the biotelemetry transmitter chain.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. **Bioelectric Source (Microphone / Piezo Pickup):** Converts heart sounds (S1 "lub" and S2 "dub") into a low-amplitude voltage signal of bandwidth 20 Hz – 2 kHz.

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128, Gain = 200):** Amplifies the differential PCG signal while rejecting common-mode noise (50 Hz mains hum). Gain is set by $R_G = 49.4 \text{ k}\Omega / (G - 1) = 49.4/199 \approx 248 \, \Omega$.

3. **DC Offset Adder:** Adds a DC voltage of $V_{DD}/2 = 4.5$ V to the amplified PCG signal so that the bipolar audio signal lies in the centre of the VCO's linear range.

4. **Low-Pass Filter (2nd Order Butterworth, $f_c = 2$ kHz):** Removes high-frequency noise above the PCG band, preventing the VCO from being modulated by out-of-band interference.

5. **VCO (IC 4046):** The centre-tapped PCG signal modulates the carrier. The VCO's output frequency is:

$$
f_{out} = f_{center} + K_0 \cdot v_{in}(t)
$$

6. **RF Buffer + Antenna:** The square-wave FM carrier drives a small loop antenna, radiating the signal.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the four requested parameters.**

**Step 1 — Carrier (Center) Frequency:**

$$
f_{center} = \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1} = \frac{1}{4.7 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 212.77 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — VCO Gain Constant** (with $V_{offset} = 0.5$ V):

$$
K_0 = \frac{1}{R_1 \, C_1 \, (V_{DD} - V_{offset})} = \frac{1}{4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times 8.5} \approx 25.03 \text{ kHz/V}
$$

**Step 3 — Peak Amplified Voltage:**

$$
V_{peak} = 200 \times 3 \text{ mV} = 600 \text{ mV} = 0.6 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency Deviation:**

$$
\Delta f = 25.03 \text{ kHz/V} \times 0.6 \text{ V} = 15.02 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Modulation Index:**

$$
\beta = \frac{15020}{2000} = 7.51
$$

**Summary Table of Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $f_{center}$ | ≈ 212.77 kHz |
| $K_0$ | ≈ 25.03 kHz/V |
| $\Delta f$ | ≈ 15.02 kHz |
| $\beta$ | 7.51 (WBFM) |
| Carson's BW | $2(15.02 + 2) = 34.04$ kHz |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to DC-offset the input.** — Applying the raw bipolar ECG directly to pin 9 will clip the VCO at the lower rail. **Always add $V_{DD}/2$ before pin 9.** This single omission accounts for roughly 30 % of all "no output observed" lab failures.
>
> 2. **Confusing $f_{center}$ with $f_{max}$.** — $f_{center} = 1/(R_1 C_1)$ is the free-running frequency when $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$. $f_{max}$ is the saturation upper limit, not the formula value.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in modulation index.** — $\beta$ is **dimensionless**, so both $\Delta f$ and $f_m$ MUST be in the same unit (Hz). A common error is mixing kHz with Hz.
>
> 4. **Missing the LPF stage.** — Students often connect the pre-amp output directly to the VCO. The examiner expects a 1st or 2nd order LPF between them with explicit $f_c$ calculation.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Carson's Rule for bandwidth.** — Part (b) of a 7-mark question almost always ends with a bandwidth calculation. Memorise $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_{m,max})$.
>
> 6. **Not labelling the receiver side.** — A complete biotelemetry answer MUST show the receiver (PLL demodulator → LPF → output amplifier). Marks are deducted if the design is unidirectional.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Biotelemetry** = remote measurement + transmission of biological signals.
- [x] **IC 4046** = CMOS PLL containing a **VCO** + two phase comparators; we use **only the VCO** for this experiment.
- [x] **VCO center frequency formula:** $f_{center} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 \, C_1}$ (canonical, assumes $V_{DD} \gg V_{offset}$).
- [x] **Pin 9** = VCO control voltage input; **Pin 4** = VCO output (FM carrier).
- [x] **R1 is connected to Pin 11**, **C1 is connected to Pins 6–7** to ground.
- [x] **DC offset of $V_{DD}/2$ is mandatory** before pin 9 to keep the VCO in its linear region.
- [x] **ECG bandwidth** = 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz; **PCG bandwidth** = 20 Hz – 2 kHz.
- [x] **Three mandatory Tx stages:** Instrumentation Amplifier → LPF → VCO (IC 4046).
- [x] **VCO Gain:** $K_0 = \dfrac{1}{R_1 C_1 (V_{DD} - V_{offset})}$ in Hz/V.
- [x] **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = K_0 \cdot V_{peak}$, where $V_{peak} = G_{pre} \cdot A_m$.
- [x] **Modulation index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_{m,max}}$; for biotelemetry, $\beta \gg 1$ (Wideband FM).
- [x] **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2 (\Delta f + f_{m,max})$ — the IF bandwidth required at the receiver.
- [x] **Receiver side uses a second 4046 (or LM565) as a PLL demodulator** followed by an LPF and output amplifier.
- [x] **FM is preferred over AM** for biotelemetry because of superior noise immunity and SNR.
- [x] **Practical lab values:** $R_1 \sim 10$ kΩ, $C_1 \sim 1$ nF give $f_c \sim 100$ kHz — ideal for oscilloscope observation.
- [x] **ECG signals are mV-level; PCG signals from microphones are also low amplitude** — pre-amplification is non-negotiable.
- [x] **Typical INA128 gain equation:** $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_G}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Viva Tip:** If the examiner asks *"What happens if $R_1$ is increased?"*, the answer is: *The VCO center frequency decreases inversely (since $f_c = 1/R_1 C_1$), and simultaneously $K_0$ decreases, leading to reduced frequency deviation and lower modulation index.* Always present the dual effect.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Electro Surgical Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
